Form (pass/fail, bands, percentiles) shapes self-concept; pupils describe themselves in its terms. Context makes the same score feel triumphant or shameful depending on set placement. Schools attach moral worth to success, so struggle reads as a verdict on character. Teachers are game designers: pupils who repeatedly lose internalize loser identities and behave accordingly, driven either toward instrumental optimization of the system or toward deliberate avoidance. Assessment choices have consequences for who pupils become.

quote: Students learn more readily when they can connect what they are learning to what they already know. However, instructors should not assume that students will immediately or naturally draw on relevant prior knowledge.
author: Susan A. Ambrose et al.
work: How Learning Works
expand: Students learn more readily when they can connect what they are learning to what they already know. However, instructors should not assume that students will immediately or naturally draw on relevant prior knowledge. Instead, they should deliberately activate students' prior knowledge to help them forge robust links to new knowledge.

quote: The equity problem with contextualised assessment is not that disadvantaged students are less intelligent. It is that unfamiliar contexts impose a dual cognitive load: process the context and retrieve the concept simultaneously.
author: 100% Assessment
work: The Use of Context in Assessment
expand: This article applies Karl Maton's Legitimation Code Theory -- specifically his concept of semantic gravity -- to assessment design, arguing that the choice of context in an assessment question is not neutral but determines what cognitive move students must perform and which students are structurally advantaged. Semantic gravity describes the degree to which knowledge is tied to a specific context (high) or abstracted from context (low). A contextualised question (force applied to a shopping trolley) requires students to perform context-stripping before applying abstract knowledge -- an additional cognitive move that is itself a teachable skill, not a natural advantage. The article introduces 'semantic waves' -- the pedagogically productive oscillation between abstract principles and concrete examples. Good teaching rides semantic waves deliberately: moving from concept to context and back. Assessment designers should understand which position on the semantic gravity spectrum their tasks occupy and what cognitive move they are actually testing. The practical implication is that teachers should explicitly teach context-stripping as a skill. Students who have been taught to recognise the abstract principle hiding inside a contextualised question perform significantly better on contextualised assessments than students who encounter them cold.

quote: Subitizing is a low-key subconscious operation, while counting provokes significant cerebral activity.
author: David A. Sousa
work: How the Brain Learns Mathematics
expand: These results suggest that subitizing is a low-key subconscious (neuroscientists call it preattentive) operation, while counting provokes significant cerebral activity. The eyes can immediately detect the difference between two and three items without counting.

quote: The original letter clearly signaled the ways a student could opt out, but didn't clearly explain the benefits.
author: Chad Aldeman
work: Congratulations! Your Child Belongs in Algebra
expand: Chad Aldeman praises Fairfax County Public Schools for automatically enrolling more 8th graders in Algebra I Honors, but argues their parent communications undermined the initiative by focusing on risk and opt-out rather than celebrating student capability. He brings in communications expert Michelle Lerner to draft a model letter that personalizes the message, leads with congratulations, and frames acceleration as an opportunity rather than a burden. The revised letter centers the student by name, explains the value of early algebra for future coursework, and removes barriers to enrollment. The article demonstrates that even well-designed equity policies can be weakened by poor messaging, and that school districts owe families communication that matches the ambition of their programs. Aldeman's broader point is that messaging algebra acceleration as a default good—rather than a risky experiment—sends a fundamentally different signal about what schools believe students can do.

quote: Emotions take precedence over all other brain processes; the agree/disagree statements you open a lesson with will evoke emotions in most students and help them understand the concepts being shared.
author: Marilee Sprenger
work: How to Teach So Students Remember
expand: The agree/disagree statements will evoke emotions in most students. They also help them understand the concepts that are being shared. Advance organizers call on prior knowledge. Mental Note: Emotions take precedence over all other brain processes.

quote: Schools and districts have tools available to address one of their biggest challenges. But students have to actually use those tools to see any benefits.
author: Chad Aldeman
work: Ed Tech Can Be Good, Too
expand: Chad Aldeman examines a mini-study of Khan Academy used as a supplemental math tool in 13 Newark, New Jersey schools over three years (approximately 6,700 students, 87% qualifying for free/reduced lunch). The results are notable on several fronts: 70% of students met the recommended usage threshold of 30 minutes daily -- far above the typical 'five percent problem' -- average math gains were small but positive, gains scaled linearly with engagement, and most strikingly, the largest gains accrued to the lowest-performing students. Aldeman argues this finding directly addresses post-pandemic achievement recovery, since low-performing students have been hardest hit. The key insight is not that Khan Academy is uniquely powerful, but that when engagement is high, even widely-criticized tools deliver measurable outcomes. Key Points * The 'five percent problem' -- the observation that most students don't engage with ed tech apps enough to see benefits -- was largely avoided: 70% of Newark students met recommended usage thresholds. * Returns were linear: students who used Khan Academy more and completed more lessons learned more -- confirming engagement as the fundamental variable in ed tech effectiveness. * Most important finding: the largest math gains by far were for level 1 (lowest-performing) students, directly addressing the group most severely affected by pandemic learning loss. * The study challenges reflexive ed tech dismissal by showing that the critical variable is implementation quality and actual use, not the technology's intrinsic power. * 87% of the 6,700 students qualified for free or reduced-price lunch -- making the engagement rates and outcomes especially notable for high-need populations. * Aldeman concludes: 'Schools and districts have tools available to address one of their biggest challenges. But students have to actually use those tools to see any benefits.'

quote: We perform better on tests when in the same state of mind as when we studied.
author: Benedict Carey
work: How We Learn
expand: Studying while seriously impaired is wasted time, in more ways than one, as millions of students have learned the hard way. Yet, generally speaking, we perform better on tests when in the same state of mind as when we studied. Moods, preoccupations, and perceptions matter, too: how we feel while studying, where we are, what we see and hear.

quote: Students don't absorb new information in a vacuum -- they connect it to what they already know. A well-sequenced, knowledge-rich curriculum is essential.
author: Chad Aldeman
work: How Students Learn
expand: Chad Aldeman summarizes nine key takeaways from the updated Deans for Impact 'Science of Learning' report, translating cognitive science findings into practical implications for teachers, parents, and coaches. The report, developed with educational psychologists and now in its second edition, covers topics from prior knowledge and working memory limits to retrieval practice, feedback, and the persistence of myths like learning styles. Aldeman's synthesis highlights that strategies producing the strongest long-term retention -- spaced practice, retrieval, interleaving -- feel harder and less satisfying in the moment, creating a systematic bias toward less effective study approaches. He also notes that motivation need not precede learning: success often generates motivation, meaning teachers can build engagement through well-designed instruction rather than manufactured interest.

quote: Some difficulties that elicit more effort and that slow down learning will more than compensate for their inconvenience by making the learning stronger, more precise, and more enduring.
author: Peter C. Brown, Henry L. Roediger III & Mark A. McDaniel
work: Make It Stick
expand: We're interested in her jump school training because it's a great example of how some difficulties that elicit more effort and that slow down learning—spacing, interleaving, mixing up practice, and others—will more than compensate for their inconvenience by making the learning stronger, more precise, and more enduring. Short-term impediments that make for stronger learning have come to be called desirable difficulties.

quote: Project-based learning can be valuable when students already possess a strong foundation of knowledge and skills. Too often, however, schools treat projects as a substitute for content rather than an opportunity to apply content.
author: Chad Aldeman
work: In the Age of AI Knowledge Matters More Not Less
expand: Chad Aldeman identifies an internal contradiction in AFT President Randi Weingarten's AI-in-education speech, which simultaneously called for more project-based learning AND stronger foundational literacy, numeracy, and civic knowledge -- ideas that are in tension, not complementary. Aldeman argues that the progressive education argument ('facts are cheap, look them up') has been taken to its logical extreme by AI, which can now look up everything instantly -- and this actually makes foundational knowledge more important, not less. Drawing on Willingham's 2020 research in the AFT's own publication, Aldeman argues that domain knowledge is what enables critical thinking: abstract critical-thinking skills disconnected from content knowledge cannot substitute for actual fluency. The students who will benefit most from AI are those who know enough to ask good questions, evaluate answers, and build on what they learn -- not those who know the least.

quote: Creative thinking lessons build on critical thinking and go beyond simple recall to consider "what if" possibilities and incorporate real-life problem solving.
author: Patti Drapeau
work: Sparking Student Creativity
expand: Creative instruction can be used to promote achievement across content areas, establish long-term learning, encourage creative thinking and problem solving, and foster motivation and engagement. Creative thinking lessons build on critical thinking and go beyond simple recall to consider "what if" possibilities and incorporate real-life problem solving; they require students to use both divergent and convergent thinking.

quote: But as homework demands started to drop off, so did student achievement scores. While fewer students were being given homework, the scores for the lowest achievers fell particularly fast.
author: Aldeman, Chad
work: The Rise and Fall of Homework
expand: Homework rates in American schools have undergone dramatic swings over the past four decades, and Chad Aldeman uses longitudinal NCES survey data to trace those swings and interrogate their implications for student achievement. Among 9-year-olds, the percentage reporting no homework fell sharply from 35% in 1984 to 18% by 2008 — and then reversed, rising back to 34% by the most recent data, while the percentage completing one-plus hours of homework per night also declined substantially. Thirteen-year-olds have followed a steadier but similarly downward trajectory. Aldeman connects these trends to a broader erosion of academic accountability: as states have weakened graduation standards, reduced school accountability requirements, and responded to equity critiques of homework, expectations on students have quietly collapsed. The troubling correlation is that homework levels and math achievement scores moved in parallel — rising together through the 1990s and falling together thereafter, with the steepest declines concentrated among the lowest achievers. While careful not to assert direct causation from correlational data, Aldeman argues that this parallel is hard to ignore and that the evidence supports some homework — particularly the reinforcement variety — as producing genuine learning gains and better habit formation. He points to modern digital platforms like ASSISTments, Zearn, and Khan Academy as examples of how homework can be redesigned for the current era rather than simply eliminated.

quote: Human learning is a slow process that can happen over months and years rather than hours or days.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning and the Science of How We Learn
expand: Learning requires time, effort, and motivation. The necessary ingredients are (a) time, (b) goal-orientation, (c) supportive feedback, (d) accumulated successful practice, and (e) frequent review. Notions such as instant experts, superfast learning, and other magic-like programs amount to faddish quackery in violation of known and validated principles of human learning.

quote: Subjective acceleration choices, like those depending strictly on teacher recommendation, leave too much room for bias and unchecked discretion.
author: Chad Aldeman
work: When Should You Accelerate Your Child?
expand: Chad Aldeman examines Fairfax County Public Schools' automatic-enrollment policy for algebra acceleration, which projects 84.5% of 8th graders in Algebra I or higher by next year -- far above national averages. Using Virginia SOL end-of-course exam data, he shows that pass rates are high across accelerated cohorts: 91.9% for 8th-grade Algebra I takers, 98.9% for 7th-graders, and 100% for 6th-graders. Aldeman invites guest commenter Pamela Hobart to explain why automatic opt-out enrollment policies are superior to recommendation-based systems: subjective gating leaves too much room for teacher bias and anti-acceleration defaults, particularly harming girls and students of color. Research cited by Hobart finds that students who don't take Algebra 1 by 8th grade rarely catch up -- top scorers who took it as 8th-graders were 3x to 55x more likely to pursue chemistry and physics than equally capable peers who waited. Aldeman's verdict: Fairfax is doing something clearly right, but the district's opt-out letter subtly undermines the policy by framing acceleration as a risk rather than an opportunity. Key Points * Fairfax County projects 84.5% of 8th graders in Algebra I or higher by next school year, with Virginia's statewide end-of-course exams providing an accountability backstop on quality. * Pass rates strongly suggest districts are underaccelerating: 91.9% of 8th-grade Algebra takers passed, 98.9% of 7th-graders, and 100% of 6th-graders -- with almost certainly some selection effects. * Automatic opt-out policies remove teacher anti-acceleration bias; subjective recommendation systems rarely self-correct because it's more noticeable when a student is moved up than quietly kept behind. * Students who don't take Algebra 1 by 8th grade rarely catch up: top scorers accelerated to 8th-grade algebra were 3x to 55x more likely to take chemistry and physics than equally capable peers who waited. * Fairfax's opt-out letter subtly undermines the spirit of Virginia's HB2868 by framing the accelerated placement as a 'risky bet requiring sophisticated discernment' rather than earned opportunity.

quote: Surface learning is not shallow learning. It is not about rote skills and meaningless algorithms.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning for Mathematics
expand: Let us be clear: surface learning is not shallow learning. It is not about rote skills and meaningless algorithms. It is not prioritizing superficial learning or low-level skills over higher order skills. Surface learning begins with developing conceptual understanding, and then, at the right time, labels and procedures are explicitly introduced to give structure to concepts.

quote: Engelmann is best understood as last, not first. His methods are invaluable in certain circumstances. But they only become applicable after a series of much harder decisions have already been made.
author: Becky Allen
work: Engelmann Last
expand: A nuanced critique of Engelmann's Direct Instruction framework arguing that while his theory of instructional communication is powerful for well-defined domains, it only solves the problem of how to present material clearly. The harder work—deciding what knowledge to teach, how to decompose it into teachable units, which simplifications to accept, and how to build narrative structures—happens upstream of Engelmann's framework. Allen distinguishes between domains where knowledge decomposes cleanly (early literacy, arithmetic) where Engelmann excels, and more contextual domains (history, economics) where curriculum design remains the primary intellectual challenge.

quote: Any lesson planning must begin with a deep understanding of what each student already knows and can do, and how the instruction is aimed at increasing the progress and levels of achievement for each student.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning for Teachers
expand: This allows the teacher to tailor the lesson, so that the student can bridge the gap between his or her current knowledge and understanding, and the target knowledge and understanding. The primary concern is to add value to all students, wherever they start from.

quote: The trouble with instruction is that it is cheap to propose and expensive to verify.
author: Becky Allen
work: How Teaching Might Finally Learn
expand: Allen argues that the core problem in education improvement is not a shortage of instructional ideas -- AI has only multiplied those -- but a verification problem: we lack cheap, reliable ways to test which of many plausible instructional decisions actually produce better learning. Every instructional choice (splitting vs. inspection for factoring, planetary vs. energy-level model for atoms, sequential vs. comparative history teaching) is a wager on things we cannot directly observe, and the profession has been bad at discovering and preserving what works. Allen's conditional optimism is that LLM-supported online teaching may finally make it economically viable to test thousands of small instructional decisions at scale and gradually build a more cumulative science of how particular ideas are best taught. This is not an argument that LLMs possess pedagogical theory or that online testing reduces education to optimisation -- it is the modest claim that the economics of educational verification may be changing. Allen is careful to distinguish her position from naive enthusiasm: the verification problem is real and cannot be wished away with dashboards or bigger models.

quote: The notion that increasing achievement is a function of our efforts and interest is critical to success — there is no point in investing in study or preparation if we do not believe that our efforts can make a difference.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning: A Synthesis of Over 800 Meta-Analyses Relating to Achievement
expand: This sense of control over one's learning has been often studied. Students who take on personal responsibility for life events such as learning can be labeled internals. The notion that increasing achievement is a function of our efforts and interest is critical to success — there is no point, for example, in investing in study or preparation if we do not believe that our efforts can make a difference.

quote: Höffler and Leutner found a large effect for spatial content and almost nothing for declarative content. 'Video is engaging' does not explain that pattern. An actual explanation would.
author: Becky Allen
work: Plausible Stories Are Not Explanations
expand: Becky Allen examines the question of whether AI-generated video can improve learning, and uses it as a case study in how educators mistake plausible mechanisms for actual explanations. Drawing on David Deutsch's criterion that a good explanation must be 'hard to vary' — its components cannot be changed without breaking the explanation — Allen argues that most claims about educational technology offer stories that are easy to vary, and therefore are not genuine explanations at all. She applies Höffler and Leutner's meta-analysis, which found a strong effect of animation for spatial/procedural content (d=1.06) but near-zero effects for declarative content, to show that 'video is engaging' explains nothing. She closes with a warning about the fluency trap: as AI lowers the production cost of educational video, the volume of plausible-but-untested material will increase, and educators will need stronger epistemic standards to resist it.

quote: The more words we have, the more packets of knowledge, and, hence, the more background knowledge we have.
author: Robert J. Marzano
work: Building Background Knowledge for Academic Achievement
expand: The actual words we know are tags or labels for our packets of knowledge. Thus it makes intuitive sense that the more words we have, the more packets of knowledge, and, hence, the more background knowledge we have. The understanding that a word is the representation for a packet of knowledge enhances our understanding of vocabulary and greatly expands its usefulness.

quote: For the first time, we have tools with the right architecture to do remediation the way tutors actually do it. Not by finding the right pre-built chunk, but by not chunking at all.
author: Becky Allen
work: Teaching Backwards From the Mind
expand: Teaching backwards—focused remediation through dialogue rather than curriculum chunks—works fundamentally differently from initial classroom instruction. Drawing from her experience as both classroom teacher and private tutor, Becky Allen demonstrates that effective gap-filling operates outside curriculum structures entirely. The critical insight is that tutoring succeeds because it works from the student's distributed understanding of their misconception, meeting their gap on its own terms rather than routing it through predetermined curriculum sequences. Modern language models are architecturally better suited to this dialogue-based diagnostic approach than traditional adaptive learning platforms, which force errors back through curriculum-shaped pipes. The optimal remediation space lies between small conceptual holes (best served by flexible dialogue) and massive gaps (which require structured initial instruction); for the middle range, conversational AI tutoring shows promise of doubling learning impact compared to human tutoring alone.

quote: One of the greatest obstacles to learning is the fear of making mistakes. Because many students equate making mistakes with feeling humiliated, they will avoid learning tasks that appear too challenging.
author: Tomlinson & Sousa
work: Differentiation and the Brain
expand: Fear is an intense emotional response that shuts down higher cognitive processes so the brain can focus on the source of the fear. One of the greatest obstacles to learning is the fear of making mistakes. Because many students equate making mistakes with feeling humiliated, they will avoid learning tasks that appear too challenging. Effective teachers know that to prevent this, it is best to openly address these fears with students.

quote: The performance is visible. The underlying capacity may not be. Gilbert Ryle made a similar distinction between reciting multiplication facts and calculating them: the same answer can conceal different capacities.
author: Becky Allen
work: The Reality of Understanding
expand: Becky Allen argues, drawing on David Deutsch's The Beginning of Infinity, that educational constructs like 'understanding,' 'fluency,' and 'misconception' are genuinely real abstractions—not because they can be directly observed, but because they are part of our best explanations for patterns of learner behavior. She opens with the central problem: identical visible performances—the same correct answer, the same well-phrased claim—can be produced by entirely different underlying capacities (retrieval, calculation, genuine understanding), and surface performance alone cannot distinguish between them. Deutsch's standard—that abstractions are real when they are part of our best explanations, like software or money—rescues educational constructs from the crude objection that they are unreal because invisible, while simultaneously raising the standard for using them. The criterion becomes explanatory necessity: 'Do I need something like understanding to explain what this learner can and cannot do, including under varied conditions?' Assessment on this account is not direct inspection of learning but the design of evidence from which we infer something about the learner's invisible capacity—making the shadows of different constructs diverge so they can be distinguished. Allen's key insight is that the problem with educational measurement is not that learning is invisible but that many different invisible things can cast the same visible shadow.

quote: Teachers need professional development because the job of teaching is so difficult, so complex, that one lifetime is not enough to master it.
author: Dylan Wiliam
work: Embedded Formative Assessment
expand: The reason that teachers need professional development has nothing to do with professional updating. As far as I am aware, there haven't been any real breakthroughs in teaching for the last two thousand years. Teachers need professional development because the job of teaching is so difficult, so complex, that one lifetime is not enough to master it.

quote: Students in 7th grade should not have to memorize the 50 states; they should already know them by then because they have had rich experiences learning about them in elementary school.
author: Lauren Brown
work: Should Students Memorize the 50 States
expand: After a 7th-grade student identified Texas only as 'that big state at the bottom with 40 electoral votes,' Lauren Brown resolved to make all her students memorize the 50 states across three sequential tests tied to US history content. She reports five lessons: every student succeeded and expressed pride in mastery; parents universally supported it rather than objecting to rote learning; contextual chunking by historical era (colonies, Missouri Compromise, Lewis and Clark, Civil War) dramatically aided retention; physical geography literacy -- oceans, mountains, rivers -- is a necessary prerequisite many students also lack; and geographic knowledge correlates with travel and family socioeconomics, creating equity gaps that schools can bridge. Brown argues states should be mastered before 7th grade through rich K-5 experiences: puzzles, songs, weather reports, science units, and historical connections -- not as emergency rote memorization in secondary school.

quote: Children — like all learners — try to make sense of what they are learning by fitting it into what they already know or believe.
author: Susan A. Ambrose
work: How Learning Works: Seven Research-Based Principles for Smart Teaching
expand: Some psychologists explain this distortion as a result of our striving for internal consistency. Vosniadou and Brewer found that children reconcile their perception that the earth is flat with formal instruction stating that the earth is round by conceiving of the earth as a pancake. In other words, children — like all learners — try to make sense of what they are learning by fitting it into what they already know or believe.

quote: The most important sentence of her presentation was, 'We can't develop what we can't name.' So carefully defining the work we do is imperative.
author: Lauren Brown
work: Takeaways from ResearchED NYC
expand: Lauren Brown reports from ResearchED NYC, synthesising key takeaways from five sessions across the day. A recurring theme is that the science of learning -- specifically how memory, knowledge-building, and deliberate practice work -- is conspicuously absent from standard teacher training, a gap Zaretta Hammond frames as a moral failure. Deans for Impact presenters demonstrate that novice teachers consistently misidentify the more cognitively effective classroom activity. Nidhi Sachdeva argues that expert teaching requires both the science of learning and the craft developed through deliberate practice, and that the science component should come first, in initial training. Brown concludes with Sachdeva's caution that professional development too often prioritises exposure over practice, leaving teachers inspired but unchanged by Monday morning.

quote: When we sleep, the brain reviews the events and tasks of the day, storing them more securely than at the time we originally processed them.
author: David A. Sousa
work: How the Brain Learns
expand: The encoding of information into the long-term memory sites occurs during sleep, more specifically during the rapid-eye movement (REM) stage. When we sleep, the brain reviews the events and tasks of the day, storing them more securely than at the time we originally processed them. What we think and talk about while awake very likely influences the nature and shape of the memory consolidation that occurs during sleep.

quote: I've often ruminated about what retrieval practice—that all-important tool in learning—looks like in history class. And one of the obvious answers is writing.
author: Lauren Brown
work: Why We All Need to Care About Handwriting
expand: Lauren Brown reports a dramatic increase in students unable to write legibly or fluently, coinciding with post-COVID reliance on Chromebooks. Cognitive science shows that handwriting activates the left hemisphere regions managing planning, monitoring, and language processing—skills essential to writing and metacognition. Note-taking remains a critical mechanism for encoding knowledge and enabling retrieval practice; students who cannot write fluently lose this cognitive tool. Direct instruction requires students to respond and write during lessons, making legible handwriting foundational to learning. The shift to devices has left many students unable to compensate through typing or handwriting, creating a literacy crisis that requires explicit intervention.

quote: Working memory is occupied by a combination of intrinsic and extraneous load. The sum of the two cannot exceed working memory capacity if learning is to take place.
author: John Sweller
work: Cognitive Load Theory in Action
expand: Working memory is occupied by a combination of intrinsic and extraneous load. The sum of the two cannot exceed working memory capacity if learning is to take place. The key idea of Cognitive Load Theory is to minimise extraneous load and optimise intrinsic load.

quote: Every time I read current arguments about history and social studies instruction, I have the same reaction: we have been here before.
author: Douglas Carnine
work: History: Teach It and Learn from It
expand: Douglas Carnine revisits a 30-year-old research program on teaching U.S. history to show that today's heated debates -- Wexler's content knowledge argument vs. Bassett/ Shiffman's critique of primary-source over-use -- were partly answered decades ago and then forgotten. Five studies using Carnine's experimental 'Understanding U.S. History' textbook found that students using it included more historical facts and concepts with fewer misconceptions in essays, scored higher on knowledge tests, learned vocabulary faster, and retained more content. The textbook was built around six design principles from Theory of Instruction: primed background knowledge, big ideas, conspicuous strategies, strategic integration, mediated scaffolding, and judicious review. The three-phase instructional sequence -- (1) Acquisition (primed background knowledge), (2) Organization (connecting facts to big ideas through strategies and scaffolding with judicious review), (3) Application (writing, problem-solving, and primary source analysis) -- shows why skipping phases predictably fails: primary sources work after preparation, not instead of it. Memorization is not the goal but is indispensable. The professional lesson: education doesn't preserve its own learning and must build cumulative knowledge bases for content areas just as it is beginning to do for literacy. Key Points * Five studies found students using the knowledge-rich, sequenced experimental textbook scored higher on knowledge tests, learned vocabulary faster, wrote essays with more facts and fewer misconceptions, and retained more content over time -- including students with learning disabilities. * The six design principles from Theory of Instruction: primed background knowledge, big ideas, conspicuous strategies, strategic integration, mediated scaffolding, and judicious review -- anticipated today's knowledge-vs-skills debate by thirty years. * Three-phase instructional sequence: Acquisition (build background knowledge first), Organization (connect facts to big ideas with scaffolding and distributed review), Application (use primary sources AFTER preparation, not instead of it). * Primary sources require preparation to work: novices who encounter primary sources without prior content knowledge experience cognitive overload, shallow analysis, and misinterpretation; historians read sources effectively because they already have rich background knowledge and interpretive routines. * The professional lesson: education repeatedly forgets what it has already learned; without cumulative knowledge bases for content areas, the field will continue to rediscover old insights and reinvent the wheel.

quote: It is common knowledge that "too much" emotion can prevent clear thinking; but too little emotion can be associated with low engagement.
author: Michael Baker, Jerry Andriessen & Sanna Järvelä
work: Affective Learning Together
expand: Traditionally, rational and objective thinking was given precedence, and was supposed to be radically different from "irrational" emotions. Certainly, it is common knowledge that "too much" emotion can prevent clear thinking; but too little emotion can be associated with low engagement.

quote: The period of preventable harm can be defined as the interval of time between when evidence was established and when evidence was uniformly applied by the profession.
author: Douglas Carnine
work: What Education Can Learn From Trusted Professions
expand: Douglas Carnine argues that education remains in what he terms a 'period of preventable harm' — the interval between when evidence for effective practice was established and when it is uniformly enforced — because it lacks the five structural pillars that define trusted professions: a shared knowledge base, coherent preparation systems, competence-signaling licensure, quality-protecting accreditation, and accountability guardrails. Drawing lessons from medicine, maritime navigation, and finance, he shows that each of these professions exited its period of preventable harm not through internal professional agreement but through external pressure: legislative action, public scandal, regulatory enforcement. The parallels are disturbing in scale: the forty-year delay in universally requiring handwashing after Semmelweis's 1847 proof likely cost hundreds of thousands of lives; education's failure to mandate evidence-based reading instruction since 1970 has likely exposed 60 to 70 million fourth graders to preventable reading failure. In trusted professions, local control exists inside accountability guardrails — a hospital cannot declare itself exempt from sterile technique — but in education, local preference can simply override the strongest available evidence. Carnine's prescriptive conclusion is that the fifth pillar — accountability — is the master lever, and that State Departments of Education must evolve from passive data collectors to proactive enforcers with the authority to intervene when districts persistently fail to implement evidence-based instruction.

quote: When students possess some relevant knowledge, it can lead both students and instructors to assume that students are better prepared than they truly are for a particular task or level of instruction.
author: Susan A. Ambrose et al.
work: How Learning Works
expand: Even when students' prior knowledge is accurate and activated, it may not be sufficient to support subsequent learning or a desired level of performance. Indeed, when students possess some relevant knowledge, it can lead both students and instructors to assume that students are better prepared than they truly are for a particular task or level of instruction.

quote: Active construction in sandbox games like Roblox, and immersion in narrative-rich worlds like Genshin Impact, appears to work on the mind differently than passive scrolling.
author: Cheng, Yung-hsun
work: The Impact of Online Games on Creativity and the Role of Imagination
expand: Research from Taiwan finds young people highly motivated to play online games display higher imagination and measurable creativity, with imagination mediating the games-creativity link. The findings complicate flat moral narratives about screens, showing that not all digital engagement is equivalent. Active construction in sandbox games and immersion in narrative worlds appears to work on the mind differently than passive scrolling. The posture of the self matters more than the medium.

quote: When you try to remember a list of numbers by saying them aloud, you are using a verbal memory loop, a part of immediate memory that can hold information for only about 2 seconds.
author: David A. Sousa
work: How the Brain Learns Mathematics
expand: When you try to remember a list of numbers by saying them aloud, you are using a verbal memory loop, a part of immediate memory that can hold information for only about 2 seconds. This forces you to rehearse the words to refresh them in the loop. As a result, your memory span is limited by how many number words you can say in less than 2 seconds.

quote: What is not plausible is to pin these dramatic UK, Anglosphere and global trends on a specific set of education policy decisions that only affected England.
author: Christodoulou, Daisy
work: Do Knowledge-Rich Curriculums Cause Mental Health Problems
expand: Christodoulou investigates whether England's knowledge-rich education reforms have caused mental health problems in students. Using cross-UK comparisons with Scotland and Wales, which adopted different curriculum approaches, she examines wellbeing data from the WHO's Health Behaviour in School-Aged Children survey. While England's attainment rose relative to Scotland and Wales, she finds that life satisfaction declined equally across all three nations, suggesting global forces (pandemic, social media, economic insecurity) rather than curriculum type drive youth mental health trends. The data undermines claims that knowledge-rich curricula create psychological distress.

quote: We often see what we expect to see; in this way, advance organizers are powerful instruments for focusing our students' attention.
author: Marilee Sprenger
work: How to Teach So Students Remember
expand: As Marzano, Pickering, Norford, Paynter, and Gaddy put it, "We often see what we expect to see." In this way, advance organizers are powerful instruments for focusing our students' attention. These organizers come in many packages.

quote: For very young children, there is a lot of evidence that learning relies on eye contact, shared attention, imitation, emotional connection, and interactive back-and-forth conversation.
author: Daisy Christodoulou
work: Motorways with No Speed Limits and Car-Free Town Centres
expand: Using Germany's context-dependent approach to cars -- unrestricted motorways and car-banned medieval town centres -- as a guiding metaphor, Daisy Christodoulou argues that the debate about educational technology should not be pro- or anti-tech but context-sensitive. She updates her 2020 Teachers vs Tech argument: the primary justification for ed tech is scale -- technology is a great equaliser because quality variations within schools (and across classrooms) exceed quality variations between them, a problem money and smaller class sizes have not solved. But the Covid natural experiment revealed something equally important: even inconsistent in-person schooling outperformed remote screen-based learning, strongly suggesting that learning is social and embodied in ways that screens cannot replicate. For very young children especially, learning depends on eye contact, shared attention, imitation, and interactive back-and-forth -- conditions that screens struggle to approximate. Christodoulou's conclusion: use technology to achieve scale on tasks where consistency and delivery efficiency are paramount, but protect the human, social, and physical dimensions of learning that remain irreplaceable. KEY TAKEAWAYS * The fundamental justification for education technology is scale: OECD data shows quality variations within schools exceed variations between schools -- technology can deliver consistent quality in ways that hiring more teachers or reducing class sizes cannot. * The Covid natural experiment was decisive: unlike remote working, telemedicine, and e-commerce (all of which accelerated permanently), remote learning did not displace in-person school -- parents rushed to return children and learning data confirmed they were right to. * Learning is social and embodied -- for young children, it depends on eye contact, shared attention, imitation, emotional connection, and interactive back-and-forth conversation -- conditions screens approximate poorly. * The pro-tech argument for fully personalised screen-based education fails on empirical grounds: no one has found a way to successfully educate students at scale with a human-light, tech-heavy alternative involving screens most of the time. * Context determines whether technology helps or hurts: use it where consistency and scale are the problem (the motorway); protect physical, social, human-led environments where embodied learning is essential (the town centre).

quote: Our assumptions about learning are suspect, if not wrong. Having something going on in the study environment, like music, is better than nothing.
author: Benedict Carey
work: How We Learn
expand: This research establishes a couple of points that are valuable in developing a study strategy. The first is that our assumptions about learning are suspect, if not wrong. Having something going on in the study environment, like music, is better than nothing (so much for sanctity of the quiet study room).

quote: The Trivium is the study of grammar, logic, and rhetoric, which train mind and tongue in the critical thinking, clear communication, and eloquence necessary for a well-rounded life.
author: Classical Ed Review
work: The Trivium
expand: Davis C. Smith reviews Sister Miriam Joseph's The Trivium — a dense, meticulously conceived classroom textbook first printed in 1937 and now newly available to the public — arguing it deserves far greater prominence in the classical education literature. Joseph places logic first as "the art of thinking," followed by grammar as "the art of inventing symbols and combining them to express thought," then rhetoric, an ordering that differs from the popularized Sayers model but follows its own coherent internal logic. The book functions as a near-complete Summa of the language arts, dense with rules, tables, diagrams, and quotations from the Western literary tradition that situate the Trivium in continuity with Greco-Roman, medieval, and Renaissance humanist learning. Smith notes real shortcomings: the grammar chapter neglects formal sentence analysis, rhetoric receives only brief treatment, and the prose style requires concentrated effort from the reader. Despite these limits, Smith recommends The Trivium as the best available single-volume resource for anyone who wants to speak, write, and reason at the highest level of the classical liberal arts tradition. KEY INSIGHTS • Joseph's ordering — logic, then grammar, then rhetoric — is coherent within her framework: reason directs all other acts, clear thinking must precede its symbolization in language, which must precede persuasive communication. • The book's greatest strength is its natural connection between the Trivium and literature: Joseph draws extensively from the Western literary tradition to illustrate grammatical usage, argumentation, and rhetorical devices. • The Trivium is ultimately a defense of liberal arts for human flourishing — not a utilitarian skills guide but an invitation to engage with language as a serious, dignified human activity. • Real shortcomings include: no formal sentence analysis in the grammar chapter, a novel shorthand for categorical propositions that may confuse readers, and an underdeveloped rhetoric section focused on literary analysis rather than classical oratory. • The book is most suitable for advanced college-level courses in logic or composition; most readers will need supplementary texts devoted to each of the three arts for comprehensive coverage.

quote: It's one thing to feel confident of your knowledge; it's something else to demonstrate mastery.
author: Peter C. Brown, Henry L. Roediger III & Mark A. McDaniel
work: Make It Stick
expand: It's one thing to feel confident of your knowledge; it's something else to demonstrate mastery. Testing is not only a powerful learning strategy, it is a potent reality check on the accuracy of your own judgment of what you know how to do.

quote: Book-starved and book-rich ELA classrooms can both be found in American schools. Predictably, curriculum is an essential influence.
author: Curriculum Insight Project
work: Unpacking the RAND Survey on Books in English Language Arts Curriculum
expand: A RAND survey of 3,640 ELA teachers reveals massive variance in book assignment: 41% of students have two or fewer books per year, 10% of middle school classrooms have zero assigned books, but 24% of secondary teachers assign five or more -- meaning book-starved and book-rich classrooms coexist at every demographic level. Demographics play a smaller predictive role than expected; curriculum type is the decisive variable: teachers using publisher-developed 'standards-aligned' (SA) curricula were significantly more likely to assign fewer books, with 57% of heavy SA users assigning only 1-2 books compared to 25% of non-publisher-curriculum teachers. The Curriculum Insight Project argues RAND's analysis is undermined by its reliance on EdReports as a standards-alignment proxy, since EdReports has awarded top marks to book-starved basals (Wonders, Into Reading) while giving middling or no reviews to book-rich, evidence-backed curricula like Bookworms and Fishtank. The real culprit is basals specifically -- not 'standards-aligned curricula' in the abstract -- which dominate market share partly because EdReports certification became a gateway to state-approval lists and district purchasing decisions. The article challenges RAND's call for more research as a 'shameful waste of time': the reading-volume literature is already substantial, Tim Shanahan's defense of passage-centered programs involves a conflict of interest (he co-authored Wonders and Emerge), and the precautionary principle demands whole-book curricula until passage-only instruction is affirmatively shown to cause no harm.

quote: Students with a fixed mindset do not believe effort and engagement make a difference; students with a growth mindset believe their skills and abilities can be developed.
author: Patti Drapeau
work: Sparking Student Creativity
expand: Dweck noted that students who have a fixed mindset do not believe effort and engagement make a difference; they believe they are born with a certain potential and it does not change. Students with a growth mindset, on the other hand, believe their skills and abilities can be developed. These students engage in hard work and demonstrate effort. When they make mistakes, they learn and grow from them.

quote: The most powerful thing a school can give a child is an education. We have spent too long being distracted from it.
author: Stephanie Dehghani
work: The Role of Schools Was Never the Problem
expand: This article argues against the growing educational discourse that repositions schools as primary providers of mental health support and therapeutic intervention. The author, who has worked in both underprivileged schools and curriculum leadership, insists that the framing of schools as 'community health hubs' subtly deprioritizes the one thing schools are uniquely positioned to do: teach children to read and learn. The piece draws on the Grattan Institute's 2024 Reading Guarantee report, which found one in three Australian children cannot read proficiently — largely because of ineffective teaching approaches like whole-language and balanced literacy. The author is careful not to argue against schools partnering with health services but against the 'wellbeing drift' that treats teaching as a secondary priority to emotional regulation. The core claim is that teaching children well is itself a profound act of care, and that the evidence overwhelmingly shows literacy and academic achievement are the interventions most likely to change life trajectories for disadvantaged children.

quote: Unless the material is strongly meaningful, relevant and timely, it is subject to rapid and substantial forgetting.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning and the Science of How We Learn
expand: We appear to learn specific small-scale behaviours, isolated bits of knowledge, or low-level objectives within only a few minutes. But impressions of quick learning are deceptive for many reasons. Unless the material is strongly meaningful, relevant and timely, it is subject to rapid and substantial forgetting.

quote: Productive struggle is not a feature of difficulty. It is a feature of how difficulty is designed. The same objective challenge can be productive or demoralising depending entirely on the scaffolding around it.
author: Patrick Dempsey
work: If Productive Struggle Is the Goal, You're Doing it Wrong
expand: Patrick Dempsey draws on aviation behavioral engineering research to argue that 'productive struggle' in education is frequently implemented in a way that produces unproductive frustration instead, because designers fail to make the discomfort psychologically intelligible to learners. Aviation safety researchers identified eight behavioral techniques — including occupied time, reciprocity, instrument orientation, executive function engagement, external cues, narration, and exit mode — that make difficult and tedious tasks cognitively manageable. Dempsey argues these same techniques, applied to course design, transform arbitrary difficulty into genuinely productive challenge. The core claim is that struggle becomes productive only when learners understand why they are struggling, can see their progress, and have a clear sense of where the challenge ends. Without that scaffolding, difficulty is just difficulty.

quote: It is through this early consolidation that they can begin to retrieve information efficiently, so that they make room for more complex problem solving.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning for Mathematics
expand: Surface learning of concepts and skills goes beyond just an introductory point; students need the time and space to begin to consolidate their new learning. It is through this early consolidation that they can begin to retrieve information efficiently, so that they make room for more complex problem solving.

quote: Modern education measures how well a student's brain has adapted to an environment specifically engineered against how human brains actually encode and retrieve information.
author: Patrick Dempsey
work: If You're Defending Education Against AI, You Don't Understand Learning
expand: Four words used interchangeably in education — learning, teaching, school, and education — originally implied motion and aliveness. Over time they were collapsed into a single system focused on credentialing. When people panic about AI threatening 'education,' they are actually defending the credential, the homework, and the essay — not learning itself. AI didn't create the misalignment between the system and genuine learning; it just made the problem impossible to ignore.

quote: The key issue is that children may think differently from adults, which means that attention needs to be given to how and not only to what the child is learning.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning for Teachers
expand: Based on Piaget's notions, Shayer developed a program of 'cognitive acceleration': the mind develops in response to challenge or disequilibrium, so any intervention must provide some cognitive conflict; the mind grows as we learn to become conscious of its own processes; and cognitive development is a social process promoted by high-quality dialogue among peers.

quote: Negative experiences are expensive. If a learning experience takes from students repeatedly without giving enough back, the relationship to you and the course cannot but degrade.
author: Dempsey, Patrick
work: Students Are Customers
expand: In the concluding installment of his airline-education series, Patrick Dempsey applies the Rule of Reciprocity to higher education's student motivation crisis: students are increasingly disengaged because the educational system withdraws -- through requirements, deadlines, and policies -- before making any deposits. Airlines have mastered the art of making an inherently uncomfortable experience survivable through choreographed reciprocity (greetings, maps, snacks, narration during turbulence), and education needs to learn the same lesson. Dempsey invokes Alex Hormozi's 'give, give, give, ask' principle and Gottman's 5:1 positive-to-negative interaction ratio to argue that course design failure is fundamentally about inverted deposit-withdrawal ratios, not about student laziness or surveillance gaps. The article's honest acknowledgment that no one can be certain which cognitive skills will matter in five to twenty years makes the reciprocity argument more urgent: if teachers cannot be sure the content is essential, the relational investment must be non-negotiable. AI has accelerated the credibility problem: students can now see in real time that AI can do the assigned work, so the only remaining argument for compliance is relational, not instrumental. The article closes by noting that traditional higher ed's structural props -- legacy prestige, credential requirements, family conviction in degrees -- are eroding, and the underlying challenge of student belief is arriving faster than institutions can adapt.

quote: Achievement is more likely to be increased when students invoke learning rather than performance strategies, accept rather than discount feedback, and benchmark to difficult rather than to easy goals.
author: John Hattie
work: Visible Learning: A Synthesis of Over 800 Meta-Analyses Relating to Achievement
expand: It is more likely that there are stronger relationships between certain self-strategies and achievement. Achievement is more likely to be increased when students invoke learning rather than performance strategies, accept rather than discount feedback, benchmark to difficult rather than to easy goals, compare themselves to subject criteria rather than to other students, and effect self-regulation and personal control rather than learned helplessness.

quote: If you want to test the effect of a true phone ban on learning, phones likely need to be genuinely removed from the school environment bell-to-bell.
author: The Digital Delusion
work: Researchers Say Phone Bans Dont Improve Learning But They Didnt Study Bans
expand: A new Stanford/UPenn working paper on 'school phone bans' is drawing headlines claiming bans do not improve learning -- but The Digital Delusion argues the study never examined an actual ban. The intervention studied was Yondr pouches: lockable sleeves students carry on their person, which reduced phone activity by approximately 17%. A phone in a pouch the student still carries is not equivalent to a phone removed from campus. This follows a 2024 paper that similarly attracted 'bans are ineffective' press coverage despite only studying softer classroom-use policies. The author maintains that the negative effects of digital technology on learning -- craving, fragmented consolidation, energetic depletion -- extend throughout the school day, not just during class, meaning partial measures cannot constitute a genuine test. The paper itself has not been peer reviewed, and its phone-activity measure is inferred from location data rather than direct observation. The author warns that researchers, not just journalists, are now blurring the definitional line between policy and ban -- making it harder to evaluate what genuine, bell-to-bell phone removal would actually produce. KEY TAKEAWAYS * The Stanford/UPenn working paper used Yondr pouches as its 'phone ban' intervention -- but students kept the pouches on their persons throughout the day, and the resulting reduction in phone activity was only about 17%, far short of a genuine ban. * A phone stored in a pouch a student continues carrying is not equivalent to a phone removed from campus -- the distinction matters because the biological and cognitive mechanisms linking phones to disrupted learning operate throughout the school day, not only during class. * The 2024 predecessor paper studied classroom-use 'policies,' not bans, yet generated widespread press coverage claiming bans are ineffective -- and now a 2025 paper by researchers themselves conflates the two terms. * The 2025 paper is a working paper, not peer reviewed; its phone-activity measure is inferred from location-based behavioral data rather than directly observed phone use. * To genuinely test the effect of a phone ban on learning, phones likely need to be removed from the school environment bell-to-bell -- neither paper has studied that condition, yet both have been cited as evidence against bans.

quote: The research and theory strongly suggest that teaching vocabulary is synonymous with teaching background knowledge.
author: Robert J. Marzano
work: Building Background Knowledge for Academic Achievement
expand: Understanding the relationship between vocabulary and background knowledge provides a useful perspective on programs that seek to enhance academic background knowledge. Specifically, the research and theory strongly suggest that teaching vocabulary is synonymous with teaching background knowledge. The packets of information that constitute our background knowledge all have labels associated with them.

quote: I keep returning to chess because it illustrates something important about mathematics learning: expertise depends heavily on automaticity. What becomes automatic frees up mental space for what actually matters.
author: Edulore
work: Chess Masters and Maths Fluency
expand: Edulore uses chess expertise research to illuminate how mathematical fluency works: expert performance depends not on calculating more options but on automaticity -- the ability to recognize familiar patterns instantly from long-term memory. Adriaan de Groot's research found that strong chess players don't analyze dramatically more moves than weaker players; they recognize thousands of familiar board configurations almost instantly. Magnus Carlsen describes his style as intuitive rather than deeply calculational. This parallels mathematics learning: when basic procedures become automatic, working memory is freed for reasoning, problem-solving, and complex thinking. The article connects this to cognitive load theory, arguing that fluency is not a lower-order skill that displaces understanding but a prerequisite that enables it -- freeing working memory for the thinking that actually matters.

quote: Fear is an intense emotional response that shuts down higher cognitive processes so the brain can focus on the source of the fear and decide how to deal with it.
author: Tomlinson & Sousa
work: Differentiation and the Brain
expand: Fear is an intense emotional response that shuts down higher cognitive processes so the brain can focus on the source of the fear and decide how to deal with it. Deciding how to manage fear is the responsibility of the brain's frontal lobe. In children and adolescents, the frontal lobe is not fully developed, so it has limited ability to interpret and dampen the fear response.

quote: Pupils with higher prior attainment in maths who were taught in mixed-attainment maths classes made less progress than those taught in setted maths classes. (Equivalent to about two months' less progress.)
author: EEF Editorial Team
work: Evidence Edit Attainment Grouping and Independent Learning
expand: The EEF's May 2026 Evidence Edit presents a new UCL Institute for Education study on maths attainment grouping across 97 English schools, providing the most current evidence from the English system on a perennially contested question. The study found that mixed-attainment maths classes produce one month's less progress on average compared to setted classes, with the negative effect concentrated entirely among high-prior-attainment pupils (two months' less progress); pupils with lower prior attainment and socio-economically disadvantaged pupils made similar progress in either grouping arrangement. The newsletter also presents the EEF's seven-step model for building genuine independence: activating prior knowledge, explicit strategy instruction, modelling thinking and processes, checking understanding, guided practice, independent practice, and structured reflection—framing independence as a deliberately transferred responsibility, not a state achieved by leaving pupils to work alone. An implementation guidance section emphasizes that what ultimately matters is how an approach manifests in day-to-day school work, not just its theoretical promise. A new large-scale wellbeing trial is announced: Place2Be's Learning Together for Mental Health, a whole-school programme targeting secondary schools across England.

quote: No teacher is so good—or so bad—that he or she cannot improve. That is why we need professional development.
author: Dylan Wiliam
work: Embedded Formative Assessment
expand: Working out which teachers are good and which teachers are not so good is of far less interest to me than helping teachers improve. No teacher is so good—or so bad—that he or she cannot improve. That is why we need professional development.

quote: Children are not fragile information processors waiting to be protected from difficulty. They are unusually exploratory, socially attuned, and cognitively plastic.
author: Peter Ellerton
work: Beyond Cognitive Load Theory
expand: Ellerton argues that Cognitive Load Theory (CLT), positioned in K-12 discourse as the Science of Learning, has been stretched far beyond its evidential and conceptual limits. CLT began as a narrow theory of instructional efficiency for novices acquiring tightly structured knowledge, not a comprehensive philosophy of education, and treating it as one is a category error. Drawing on a recent Brain Sciences paper, Ellerton brings in cognitive reserve and brain reserve from neuroscience to argue that working memory emerges from dynamic neural networks shaped by emotion, motivation, fatigue, and prior experience, making over-minimised classrooms developmentally counterproductive. He flags what CLT leaves out, emotion, sociality, and self-regulation, which neuroscience treats as constitutive rather than peripheral to learning. The piece pushes back on the rigid intrinsic/extraneous/germane load distinction, noting that the brain's default mode network requires the very mind-wandering and conflict CLT pedagogy aggressively suppresses. The closing alternative reframes load as something to be adaptively managed rather than uniformly minimised, with challenge as a resource and education aimed at cultivating agency rather than optimising instruction for the next test.

quote: A single correction or refutation is unlikely to be enough to help students revise deeply held misconceptions; guiding students through conceptual change takes time, patience, and creativity.
author: Susan A. Ambrose
work: How Learning Works: Seven Research-Based Principles for Smart Teaching
expand: In some cases, inaccuracies can be corrected simply by exposing students to accurate information and evidence that conflicts with flawed beliefs. However, it is important for instructors to recognize that a single correction or refutation is unlikely to be enough to help students revise deeply held misconceptions. Instead, guiding students through a process of conceptual change is likely to take time, patience, and creativity.

quote: Intelligence is usually treated as a general cognitive capacity. People often think of it as something you're born with: the mental horsepower that allows you to process information quickly, recognise patterns or make connections.
author: Peter Ellerton
work: Critical Thinking, Expertise and Intelligence
expand: Peter Ellerton distinguishes three often-conflated concepts: intelligence (general cognitive capacity), expertise (high-level domain performance through deliberate practice), and critical thinking (learnable disciplinary reasoning norms). Critical thinking is the most actionable in education—students can be taught to recognize valid inferences, evaluate evidence, and question assumptions. Expertise develops through deliberate practice paired with critical reflection on one's own performance. Teaching critical thinking explicitly—identifying reasoning patterns, standards of argument, and conceptual distinctions—transforms how students approach learning across disciplines. Critical thinking acts as a driver of expertise development, making it essential for educators to stop treating thinking ability as fixed intelligence.

quote: Merely expecting that a memory will be needed for a future test determined whether or not sleep significantly benefitted the consolidation of that memory.
author: David A. Sousa
work: How the Brain Learns
expand: In a series of experiments, Wilhelm and his colleagues found that merely expecting that a memory will be needed for a future test determined whether or not sleep significantly benefitted the consolidation of that memory. The researchers told one group they would be tested 10 hours later; the control group got a surprise test. Sleep improved retention only in the first group, who spent more time in deep REM sleep.

quote: When learning a new language, nothing can make you feel like you are making progress more rapidly than to attack something in a new language with which you are already familiar in your old one.
author: Andrew Ellison
work: Born Again, A Language Odyssey
expand: Language learning becomes truly miraculous not at first acquisition but in second-language and continued learning, where the already-conscious mind can witness its own transformation from incomprehension to understanding. The key to keeping our relationship with language vital and alive is repeatedly encountering the familiar in new linguistic contexts, forcing ourselves to be born again into language. When learners approach new languages through material already deeply known to them, their existing knowledge transcends weak language skills while new vocabulary becomes inferrable from context, creating explosive gains in both comprehension and linguistic fluency.

quote: The primary difference between novices and experts in a given domain is that experts possess a greater amount of relevant domain-specific knowledge.
author: John Sweller
work: Cognitive Load Theory in Action
expand: In any biologically secondary area, the major, possibly sole difference between novices and experts consists of differential knowledge held in long-term memory. The primary difference between novices and experts in a given domain is that experts possess a greater amount of relevant domain-specific knowledge.

quote: THINK OF SOME WORD THAT STARTS WITH THE SAME FIRST LETTER THAT YOU SEE HERE. IT'S PROBABLY CLOSE ENOUGH.
author: Andrew Ellison
work: Search Your Feelings
expand: Andrew Ellison uses a viral TikTok video of high school students struggling to read aloud and explain a simple sentence as a lens for examining the legacy of Ken Goodman's Three Cues method in American education. Goodman's approach, pioneered in a 1967 paper, deprioritized phonics in favor of context and 'sense-making,' effectively teaching children that a word approximating the context is good enough. Ellison traces how this produced generations of students who guess rather than decode -- and how Goodman's own stated indifference to word-level accuracy ('the purpose is to make sense') reveals a philosophical incoherence that has persisted in school districts for decades. The essay blends sharp humor with serious analysis, connecting the TikTok video directly to root causes in reading science denialism. Ellison is a senior writer for Cana Academy and VP of Enrollment Management at the University of Dallas. Key Points * A viral TikTok video shows high school students at a science/technology charter school unable to correctly read aloud or explain simple sentences -- evidence of deep, systemic instructional failure. * Ken Goodman's Three Cues method (1967) taught children to use context, picture cues, and syntax to guess words rather than decode through phonics -- effectively saying approximate matches are acceptable. * Students trained via Three Cues reconstruct meaning instead of reading text; one student in the video substitutes an entire interpretation that 'makes sense' while missing the actual words. * Goodman in a 2019 interview: 'The purpose is not to learn words. The purpose is to make sense.' He also claimed a horse and pony are 'the same concept.' * Three Cues has been scientifically debunked for decades, yet its shadow looms long -- the methods persist because they were adopted system-wide and are ideologically resistant to revision. * Effective remediation requires rigorous repetitive training: 'do it on the spot and unexpectedly in the middle of class... Just keep doing it over and over, and do not for one second balk because you are afraid of making struggling students feel uncomfortable.'

quote: A positive mood has been shown to be associated with greater creativity and flexibility in problem-solving, as well as better decision-making.
author: Michael Baker, Jerry Andriessen & Sanna Järvelä
work: Affective Learning Together
expand: In a review of recent research on this question, Picard and colleagues (2004) argue for emotions as being associated with different "modes of thinking". Although a positive mood is not best for all types of thinking, it has been shown to be associated with greater creativity and flexibility in problem-solving, as well as better decision-making.

quote: Educational content that respects children's growing capacity for sustained thought and genuine understanding produces better long-term results than content designed for fragmented attention.
author: Andrew Ellison
source: Classical Ed Review on Substack, December 19, 2025
work: When the Oranges Face Flies Off
expand: Ellison argues that early Sesame Street vignettes succeeded by respecting children's capacity for genuine intellectual engagement. Three-to-five minute segments on how saxophones are made or how bread is baked held attention because they were inherently interesting, not because of animation or catchy songs. Modern 'developmentally appropriate' theory claims children cannot sustain attention and need constant rapid input, but this view lacks evidence and justifies dumbing down content. Children can attend to what is genuinely worth attending to, and education that respects this capacity produces better results than education treating the child's mind as a perpetual video game.

quote: It is common for students to know facts and concepts but not know how or when to apply them.
author: Susan A. Ambrose et al.
work: How Learning Works
expand: Declarative and procedural knowledge are not the same, nor do they enable the same kinds of performance. It is common, for instance, for students to know facts and concepts but not know how or when to apply them. In fact, research on science learning demonstrates that even when students can state scientific facts, they are often weak at applying those facts to solve problems, interpret data, and draw conclusions.

quote: I perceive a popular misconception, however, in conflating retrieval practice with tests. It seems that many people forget that that things like classroom questioning and discussions lead to retrieval (if structured properly).
author: Firth, Jonathan
source: Jonathan Firth's Memory and Metacognition Updates
work: Areas of Confusion in Cognitive Science Part 1
expand: Jonathan Firth catalogues common misconceptions within cognitive science itself -- not just the well-known neuromyths of popular culture but distortions of genuinely valid ideas from within the research tradition. He invokes Haertel's concept of 'lethal mutations': modifications of a research-based idea that change it enough to harm efficacy or produce harm. Firth focuses first on retrieval practice, arguing that the idea has been so thoroughly assimilated into teacher practice that its original research basis is now misunderstood: teachers may be doing procedures labeled 'retrieval practice' that miss the essential cognitive conditions. The article also notes the left-brain/right-brain myth as a case of how real hemispheric research becomes a misleading personality categorization. This is framed as part 1 of a multi-part series, setting up more areas of confusion to follow. Firth's central concern is that the success of cognitive science in education creates its own epistemic risk: widespread adoption of labels without the understanding needed to implement the underlying principles correctly.

quote: We argue that SoTL practitioners do not need to be fluent in all areas of the science of learning. Rather, they need to be good consumers of the literature and be aware of how to translate this work into classroom behavior.
author: Firth, Jonathan
work: Should Lecturers Know How Learning Works
expand: Jonathan Firth examines a 2012 paper by Steiner and Hakala arguing that SoTL (Scholarship of Teaching and Learning) practitioners do not need fluency in the science of learning — only the ability to be "good consumers" of research. Firth pushes back, arguing that consuming research critically requires foundational knowledge, and that a "skills-over-knowledge" framing leaves educators vulnerable to acting on misconceptions and widespread myths that pervade even the published literature. He acknowledges the pragmatic constraint that most educators cannot become full experts in the learning sciences, but argues for a middle path: the level of a "well-informed generalist" who can recognize key pitfalls, engage critically with research, and participate in scholarly debates about local application. This level is both attainable for busy practitioners and essential for avoiding the discredited fads and learning myths that consume professional development time and harm student outcomes. KEY INSIGHTS • Steiner and Hakala's influential position — that SoTL practitioners need only be "good consumers" of learning research — implies educators can figure things out by intuition, which Firth characterizes as a skills-over-knowledge argument. • Research confirms that many university instructors hold active misconceptions about learning, and that learning myths are widespread in the very published literature those instructors encounter. • A pragmatic middle path — the "well-informed generalist" — is both realistic for time-pressed educators and sufficient to recognize key pitfalls without requiring full expertise. • Foundational knowledge of learning science enables educators to engage as genuinely critical consumers of research rather than passive recipients, and to participate in scholarly debates about how findings apply in their specific contexts. • Without baseline learning science knowledge, educators risk investing time in discredited fads and missing the distinction between evidence-based practice and pedagogical mythology.

quote: Prior knowledge is not just something that helps learning. It is the material that learning works with. New information becomes meaningful when it binds with something already stored in memory.
author: Efrat Furst
work: When Prior Knowledge Helps, and When It Doesn't
expand: Furst examines the 'prior knowledge paradox' -- the finding that prior knowledge strongly predicts future knowledge but does not reliably predict learning gains. Drawing on Simonsmeier's 2022 meta-analysis, Brod's 2021 commentary, and frameworks from Ambrose et al. and McNamara & McCarthy, Furst proposes a teacher-oriented sequence for understanding when prior knowledge helps or hinders. The central claim is that prior knowledge is not merely an asset but the actual material that new learning works with: new information only becomes meaningful when it binds in working memory with existing relevant knowledge. For this binding to succeed, prior knowledge must be active, relevant, accurate, and structurally coherent -- and Furst argues that structure (deep vs. shallow) should be assessed first because it dictates all other instructional decisions. The article offers a practical framework for teachers designing learning sequences, emphasising that every question about prior knowledge presupposes a clear learning goal.

quote: 54% of U.S. adults read below the equivalent of a sixth-grade level.
author: Geoghegan, Brittany
work: Bridging the Gap Between Research and Practice in Literacy Instruction
expand: 54% of U.S. adults read below a sixth-grade level; 64% of fourth graders don't read proficiently. Teacher training programs still teach discredited methods. Teachers themselves trained in whole language can't apply phonics because they never learned it. Quality professional development grounded in science-based instruction is critical immediately. Organizations like Teach Like a Champion, UFLI, and Freedom In Education researchED conferences are filling the gap.

quote: Unfortunately, it is no longer safe to assume that every applicant with a college degree or teaching credential is highly literate. According to recent data [ ],
author: Geoghegan, B.
work: Its Not Just Students Who Need to Read Books
expand: The author argues that teachers -- not just students -- need to be active readers, and proposes 'What is the best book you've read this year?' as a litmus-test interview question for prospective teachers.
