With roughly 54% of US adults reading below a sixth-grade level, and college professors lamenting the reading habits of students in education programs, the concern is not abstract: teachers who rarely read may struggle with the fluency and literacy instruction they are expected to model. Beyond basic competence, the question signals whether a candidate brings intellectual curiosity to the classroom -- a trait the author argues is contagious among students. The author also links book-reading to diversity of thought, and to humility: books require a posture of learning from others, which the best educators carry naturally. The piece is grounded in both data on adult literacy and in personal anecdote -- a library-stocking practice for four children -- that illustrates how reading cultures are transmitted across generations.

quote: Education requires time, effort (on the part of teachers and students), and patience. There is no fast-track or loophole.
author: Beanie Geoghegan
work: Stop Looking for Shortcuts in Education
expand: Geoghegan argues K-12 education keeps falling for the same cycle: a vendor pitches an "innovative" way to make learning faster or more engaging, weak and interest-funded research is produced, billions are spent, and only afterward do educators see the damage. She traces two rounds of this pattern: the whole-language detour that left a generation with poor literacy foundations, and the one-to-one device wave that has produced students who can't hold pencils, finish books, or write without AI help. The "balance" framing being offered now for screens, she argues, is the same rhetorical move that bought phonics-skeptic curricula another decade of cover. Her counter-claim is that education is the slow, effortful formation of a person through explicit instruction, repetition, and practice, with no fast-track or loophole available. The ask is for leaders at every level (boards, superintendents, principals) to stop chasing edu-fads and invest in refining what has worked for eons.

quote: Students raised within digital abundance struggle with uncertainty, solitude, and sustained attention. Their nervous systems, trained by constant stimulation, resist the slow interior work that serious reading requires.
author: Hall, Joshua
source: Los Angeles Review of Books, March 2, 2026
work: Finding Our God-Terms
expand: Joshua Hall examines literary critic Mark Edmundson's vision for recovering the spiritual and moral purpose of American universities through the concept of 'god-terms'—organizing principles that shape a person's deepest commitments. The essay explores how contemporary campuses condition students toward consumerism and careerism while the humanities struggle to maintain their formative power. Hall presents Edmundson's argument that engagement with canonical literature offers students a path toward ethical formation and self-knowledge.

quote: students should see them as recursive and interconnected, not sequential.
author: Terry Heick and Nick Potkalitsky
source: Terry Heick's blog on Substack, February 9, 2026
work: Reprocessing the Writing Process
expand: Heick and Potkalitsky reexamine the writing process, questioning whether the traditional stages (brainstorm, draft, revise, edit, publish) still serve student learning in an era of digital composition and AI. The classical process assumes linearity and finality; writers gather ideas, compose a first draft, receive feedback, and polish. But in practice, writers move nonlinearly: they revise as they go, jump between stages, and often discover their idea through the act of writing. Modern writing tools (Google Docs, collaborative editing, real-time feedback) have changed the work. And AI adds another layer: students can now collaborate with machines that suggest, generate, and expand ideas. The article argues for a reframed understanding of the writing process that honors nonlinearity, embraces AI as a thinking partner, and focuses feedback and instruction on the thinking that precedes and shapes writing. The authors propose a model that makes the invisible visible: prewriting, composition, feedback-integration, and revision are still present, but students should see them as recursive and interconnected, not sequential. The goal is not a perfect final product but a thinking trajectory—evidence of how the writer's understanding deepened. This shift makes AI use transparent and pedagogically sound: AI can assist with brainstorming, generate alternatives for consideration, or help articulate half-formed ideas. The learner's task is to evaluate, integrate, and refine what the tool produces. This is categorically different from using AI as a shortcut; it is leveraging AI to extend thinking.

quote: This is the Matthew Effect in reading, but observed at a level of granularity that the usual discussions of “word gaps
author: Carl Hendrick
work: Orthographic Skeletons Can Children Start Learning How Words Are Spelled Before Theyve Seen it in Print
expand: Hendrick explores orthographic skeletons—invisible mental structures that form when children hear new words before encountering them in print. Drawing on research by Ataman, Beyersmann, Castles, and Wegener, the article demonstrates that oral vocabulary builds spelling expectations through phoneme-grapheme knowledge, creating a neural template that facilitates reading comprehension when words are later encountered in text. Multiple studies confirm this effect in both children and adults across inflected and derived word forms. The article argues this reframes the relationship between oral vocabulary and reading ability: knowledge isn't merely built through ambient exposure but through specific interactions between phonological and orthographic knowledge. Hendrick emphasizes that vocabulary-rich children arrive at school with hidden cognitive advantages—hundreds of pre-formed orthographic representations—while vocabulary-poor children lack these scaffolds, compounding the Matthew Effect in reading. He concludes that explicit, sequential vocabulary and phonics instruction must work in concert to build orthographic skeletons effectively.

quote: A rubric is a coherent set of criteria for students' work that includes descriptions of levels of performance quality on the criteria.
author: Susan M. Brookhart
work: How to Create and Use Rubrics for Formative Assessment and Grading
expand: Unfortunately, this definition of rubric is rarely demonstrated in practice. It should be clear from the definition that rubrics have two major aspects: coherent sets of criteria and descriptions of levels of performance for these criteria.

quote: The more a teacher relied on what they learned in their teacher preparation program, the less they knew about evidence-based reading instruction.
author: Aldeman, Chad
work: The Science, and Culture, of Reading Instruction
expand: Chad Aldeman analyzes a Fordham Institute survey of 1,244 K-3 teachers that reveals both genuine progress and persistent failures in the science of reading movement: 40+ states have enacted SOR laws and LETRS-trained teachers demonstrably know and teach better, but four serious challenges remain. First, teacher preparation programs are still failing: teachers who relied most on their preservice college training scored lower on reading knowledge quizzes than those who relied on any other source -- including on-the-job learning -- meaning colleges are actively undermining the reform. Second, high-poverty schools show a larger phonics-commitment gap (43% of teachers ambivalent about phonics vs. 25% in affluent schools), meaning the students most dependent on school-delivered instruction are the least likely to receive it correctly. Third, 58% of teachers still hold an incorrect mental model of reading comprehension -- believing it is a transferable set of generic skills rather than a function of topic-specific knowledge and vocabulary -- which drives instruction away from content-rich curricula toward strategy drilling with no transfer effect. Fourth, cultural resistance is real: professional identity, pedagogical habit, and genuine belief in balanced literacy make teachers resistant to reimagining their practice, and the SOR movement's momentum is fragile enough that declaring victory prematurely could waste a rare window of reform.

quote: Feedback can be the information that drives the process, or it can be a stumbling block that derails the process.
author: Susan M. Brookhart
work: How to Give Effective Feedback to Your Students
expand: Hattie and Timperley reviewed these works to synthesize a model of feedback that focuses on its meaning. Their review used the lens of formative assessment questions: Where am I going? How am I going? Where to next?

quote: Before moving on to the 'you' stage of independent practice, Zara uses MWBs to gauge understanding. This allows her to identify if further support is needed before students begin their independent work.
author: Shaun Allison
work: The Future's Bright
expand: Shaun Allison, writing as a head teacher, describes an after-school session in which Year 2 Early Career Teachers (ECTs) presented their self-directed teaching improvement projects, demonstrating sophisticated reflective practice only a few months into the profession. Each ECT had chosen a specific instructional target -- Do Nows, cold calling pause time, mini-whiteboard routines, participation ratio, I-We-You modelling, programming scaffolds, vocabulary instruction -- and had implemented and assessed a discrete change rather than a vague improvement goal. Allison's framing is optimistic: this generation of new teachers is entering the profession with principled, evidence-informed approaches and the professional humility to test and adjust them. The piece is notable as a counterpoint to doom narratives about teacher recruitment and retention, offering instead a snapshot of what early-career professional development looks like when it is grounded in classroom evidence rather than top-down compliance.

quote: What we share makes us human, but how we differ makes us individuals.
author: Carol Ann Tomlinson
work: How to Differentiate Instruction in Mixed-Ability Classrooms
expand: Kids do have many things in common, because they are human beings and because they are all young people, but they also have important differences. What we share makes us human, but how we differ makes us individuals. In a classroom with little or no differentiated instruction, only student similarities seem to take center stage.

quote: An 80-minute lecture does not become rigorous just because there is no screen involved.
author: Kara Andy
work: Don't Confuse Nostalgia With Good Teaching
expand: Kara Andy rejects false binaries in education (screens vs. paper, old vs. new, AI vs. learning) in favor of intentional, balanced design. Neither pencil nor Chromebook is automatically rigorous; the question is whether learning is well-designed. Eighty-minute lectures lose students as much as passive screen time; good teaching requires students to think, discuss, create, revise, and reflect. AI can strengthen teacher capacity by generating differentiated texts, checks for understanding, and station materials, freeing teachers to lead discussion and conferences. Schools should fight for better instructional design (not panic), teacher training (not bans), clear AI guardrails (not avoidance), and balanced tool use (not digital overload). Students need both deep reading and research tools, both handwriting and collaborative spaces—responsive classrooms know when to use each.

quote: In reality, performance in and out of the classroom is best when the challenge is high but the pressure is low.
author: John Joseph
work: The Highly Effective Teacher: 7 Classroom-Tested Practices for Student Success
expand: Our society values high achievers, and although the effort to push students in that direction is well intended, it often results in students feeling enormous pressure to succeed at all costs. In reality, performance in and out of the classroom is best when the challenge is high but the pressure is low. When students feel safe taking risks and making mistakes, then they will, with proper guidance, challenge themselves in amazing ways.

quote: They still have nothing useful to say, but they want to be the ones to say it.
author: Greg Ashman
work: An Important and Solemn Communique From Me
expand: Ashman critiques the Australian Association for Research in Education (AARE) for losing relevance to teachers and policymakers, then attempting gatekeeping rather than reform. The AARE's communiqué redefines 'evidence' broadly to include fashionable but unrigorous approaches, dismisses work by thinktanks as lacking 'safeguards,' and pushes antiracism as unquestionable precept rather than open intellectual question. The association's real problem is its own irrelevance: grassroots movements like researchED now fill the void by actually serving teachers. Gatekeeping and redefinition of 'evidence' represent performative virtue signaling rather than commitment to rigor or relevance.

quote: A question or challenge is also an attention grabber. It engages the brain and activates the SEEKING system to problem solve or seek to find an answer.
author: Gayle Gregory & Martha Kaufeldt
work: The Motivated Brain: Improving Student Attention, Engagement, and Perseverance
expand: A question or challenge is also an attention grabber. It engages the brain and activates the SEEKING system to problem solve or seek to find an answer. Using think-pair-share to discuss possibilities in a low-risk environment is a good way to keep all brains engaged.

quote: If you begin to participate in the debate about mathematics teaching, two things quickly become apparent. The first is gaslighting. The second feature is a focus on the hocus pocus of conceptual understanding. This is a dodge.
author: Greg Ashman
work: Conceptual understanding is a myth
expand: Greg Ashman challenges one of the central claims used to defend inquiry-based mathematics instruction: that it develops 'conceptual understanding' in a way that explicit teaching cannot. He argues that when researchers attempt to actually measure conceptual understanding, it transforms into 'conceptual knowledge'—which, on close inspection, turns out to be indistinguishable from procedural knowledge. The Crooks and Alibali (2014) review of measurement attempts found the most common 'conceptual' task was reciting a definition of the equals sign, which is ironically a rote recitation task. Ashman further argues that questions designed to test conceptual understanding only work if students haven't been previously taught how to solve them—which means the measure is really about transfer of learning, not some separate cognitive faculty. Since Thorndike established that transfer depends on the degree to which contexts share common elements, the claim that conceptual understanding is a distinct and separately assessable thing dissolves. The piece concludes that rather than arguing about procedures versus concepts, educators should focus on measuring transfer of learning, which is the actual goal.

quote: No significant learning occurs without a significant relationship.
author: Kim Campbell
work: If You Can't Manage Them, You Can't Teach Them
expand: Caring, trusting relationships with students are absolutely the most important ingredient in an enjoyable, effective classroom. This includes students liking themselves and their school experiences better, learning more, and achieving at higher levels academically. As James Comer said, 'No significant learning occurs without a significant relationship.'

quote: My worry about observations and student evaluations is that they would push teachers to perform the most fashionable teaching techniques or to try too hard to ingratiate themselves with students.
author: Ashman, Greg
work: Curi
expand: Greg Ashma commentary performance seriously wh objective pe Ashman's sk while constr methodologi phone bans Ashman's ch detail -- runs

quote: If a student's whole brain is involved in learning, there isn't any mental area left over for challenging behavior.
author: Chris Biffle
work: Whole Brain Teaching for Challenging Kids
expand: Here is Whole Brain Teaching's secret, which I learned after a quarter of a century of failed experiments: if a student's whole brain is involved in learning, there isn't any mental area left over for challenging behavior. It doesn't matter what state you live in. The brain has no off switch and is constantly learning either positive or negative behaviors.

quote: However, these students did not go on to arrive at Newton's insight. Worse, as memory faded, there was a risk that the most salient idea they would hold on to from our discussion was that rockets make satellites orbit the Earth.
author: Greg Ashman
work: Honouring Student Ideas
expand: Greg Ashman critiques the pedagogical strategy of 'cognitive conflict' -- deliberately inducing student confusion to trigger conceptual change -- arguing it is both psychologically unpredictable and pedagogically counterproductive. Using his experience teaching Newton's satellite physics and an anecdote about a physics professor's manipulative telescope activity, Ashman illustrates how ego-activating pedagogy causes students to dig in rather than update their beliefs. He draws a parallel to social media arguments: once a person publicly commits to a position, they defend it with rhetorical devices rather than update it. The article distinguishes between extracting misconceptions from students (risky, dignity-threatening) versus presenting them as third-party examples the class can examine together (safe, productive). Ashman's conclusion reframes 'honouring student ideas' not as validating wrong answers but as a principled decision to protect student dignity and cognitive safety while still building accurate understanding.

quote: I followed algorithms, and churned out answers that matched those of the teacher, but I was never satisfied with the process.
author: Ed Southall
work: Yes, But Why? Teaching for Understanding in Mathematics
expand: When I first started school, I was good at maths, but I never really understood it. I followed algorithms, and churned out answers that matched those of the teacher, but I was never satisfied with the process. I knew how to check if things were correct, but it was the steps to get there that bothered me. I didn't understand them, and they were left to my imagination to try and explain.

quote: That sense of triviality leads to us dismissing the hard work that goes into developing these schemas and can cause us to assume that our students can somehow acquire them without all that work.
author: Ashman, Greg
work: The Curse of Conceptual Understanding
expand: Greg Ashman responds to Bill McCallum's critique of his earlier 'Conceptual understanding is a myth' post, using the exchange to clarify the cognitive science of schema formation and why the term 'conceptual understanding' is both philosophically slippery and pedagogically misleading. The article's central argument is that what educators call 'conceptual understanding' is not a distinct cognitive state separate from procedural knowledge -- it is the same rich schema, built through practice and integration of procedural and declarative knowledge, that allows experts to solve problems automatically. Ashman introduces the 'curse of knowledge' to explain why expert teachers mistakenly believe novices can shortcut schema-building through discovery or meaning-making: experts have forgotten the slow, effortful process that built their own schemas. The piece challenges the Illustrative Mathematics curriculum's foundational premise -- that conceptual and procedural knowledge can be developed simultaneously through discovery -- arguing this misunderstands how schemas work and why working memory constraints make discovery-first approaches inefficient for novices. Ashman ultimately argues that seeking 'understanding' before knowledge is fully automated is epistemically confused: understanding just is the automated, integrated schema, not a separate mental achievement that precedes or accompanies procedural fluency.

quote: She kept asking why, and he kept telling her how. 'There was never any room for me to figure things out or understand. I just had to learn his process, his way.'
author: Tracy Zager
work: Becoming the Math Teacher You Wish You'd Had
expand: When my mother was in algebra, my grandfather made math a battleground between the two of them. She kept asking why, and he kept telling her how. My mom sighed to me and said, 'I had no room. There was never any room for me to figure things out or understand. I just had to learn his process, his way.'

quote: If the technology only works for 5% of students, that still leaves 95% unserved. And the 5% it works for are likely the students who were already most motivated and most capable of self-directed learning.
author: Barton, Craig
work: AI in Education 6 Five Things I Learned from Dan Meyer
expand: Craig Barton (Eedi) reports on a conversation with Dan Meyer, a former math teacher now at Amplify who has both built AI features for classrooms and publicly criticized AI tutoring hype. The five takeaways reframe the debate: (1) AI tutors have failed to deliver because the decisions involved in real tutoring — when to intervene, when to hold back, what a student needs in this moment given their history — are social as well as cognitive, and no platform has solved even the basic timing problem. (2) The AI features that work make the teacher-student relationship stronger, not weaker: Amplify's Discussion Moments has AI process 35 student responses and surface four for class discussion, returning the relational and intellectual work to the teacher. (3) Students don't care about showing off for an LLM — the social incentive structure of a real classroom does motivational work no chatbot can replicate. (4) Teachers are better positioned than students to benefit from AI, because they can evaluate AI-generated outputs in ways novices cannot. (5) 95% of students are excluded from the efficacy studies that tout personalised learning gains — the students who engage are already the most motivated and capable of self-directed learning. Meyer's closing provocation: AI is all potential energy and no kinetic energy, and the conversation about it may be sustained by people whose financial interests are served by keeping it front of mind.

quote: Rather than trying to artificially pump motivation into students from the outside, let's focus on the right type of motivation: real, intrinsic motivation that is generated from within.
author: Rob Plevin
work: Motivate the Unmotivated
expand: Rewards, punishments and external control methods are 'quick fixes'. The main problem with them is that once one of these strategies wears out, you have to go in search of another. So, rather than trying to artificially pump motivation into students from the outside, let's do it properly. Let's focus on the right TYPE of motivation... real motivation, intrinsic motivation that is generated from within.

quote: You have to answer the 'can this work at all?' question before you answer the 'can this work for everyone?' question.
author: Barton, Craig
work: AI in Education 8 Five Things I Learned from Becky Allen
expand: Education researcher Becky Allen, co-founder of Teacher Tapp and a researcher at Alpha School, offers carefully calibrated optimism about AI in education. She argues AI will excel at replicating proven best practices at scale but will not discover new ways to teach, because education lacks the feedback verification loops that make reinforcement learning effective. Her five key insights cover prerequisite knowledge activation, guided worked examples, why Khanmigo failed, Alpha School as a deliberately constrained laboratory, and a prediction that mainstream schools will mostly stay the same while software-powered micro-schools emerge at the margins for students the current system cannot serve. Key Insights • AI will excel at implementing what we already know about effective teaching and delivering it consistently at scale, but education's verification problem prevents it from discovering fundamentally new teaching methods -- we cannot measure deep understanding and transfer on the timescales reinforcement learning requires. • The best current AI tutoring applications mimic great one-to-one tutors: activating prerequisite knowledge through adaptive dialogue and breaking worked examples into interactive steps that require student engagement before revealing the next stage. • Khanmigo failed because students always choose the path of least resistance; effective AI learning platforms must control the learning pathway rather than offering it as an optional chatbot the student can ignore. • Alpha School serves as a valuable benign laboratory -- testing what can work at all before asking whether it can work for everyone, in a setting where failures are less costly for vulnerable children. • Mainstream schools will mostly remain structurally unchanged; the real transformation will come through better independent learning platforms, solved marking workflows, and software-powered micro-schools serving the 20% of persistently absent students the current system cannot reach.

quote: The only way to learn mathematics is to do mathematics. You will learn about the mathematics you teach by doing the mathematics you teach.
author: Edward C. Nolan & Juli K. Dixon
work: Making Sense of Mathematics for Teaching High School
expand: 'The only way to learn mathematics is to do mathematics.' —Paul Halmos. As you read Making Sense of Mathematics for Teaching High School, you will have the chance to become the learner once again. You will learn about the mathematics you teach by doing the mathematics you teach.

quote: Normally you would turn to psychometrics to map these student behaviours to cognitive insights, but traditional psychometric methods can only be applied to simple assessment types. (Woodhead on Burstein et al.)
author: Craig Barton, Simon Woodhead, Bibi Groot
work: EEDI 3-Read Friday 110 Responsible AI LLMs as Students Screen Time
expand: EEDI's weekly roundup curated by Craig Barton with contributions from Chief Data Scientist Simon Woodhead and Chief Impact Officer Bibi Groot covers three research papers at the intersection of AI, assessment, and classroom technology. Burstein et al.'s paper on the Duolingo English Test describes a novel psychometric framework for responsible AI-assisted assessment of non-traditional response types—student dialogues, confidence levels, explanations—that conventional psychometrics cannot handle; it offers a model for validated assessment outside traditional high-stakes testing. Borchers, Vie, and Azevedo found that GPT-4.1 simulates student think-aloud reasoning poorly: LLM outputs are too long, too confident, and too coherent compared to real struggling students, because LLMs are trained on expert-authored text and structurally cannot represent novice, emotionally-charged, half-formed reasoning—creating a scaffold-fading risk in AI tutoring. Groshell's piece (separately archived) argues that blanket screen bans dodge the harder question of what students are doing on screens, risking the elimination of well-designed instructional tools because activity-based pedagogy has misused them.

quote: Choice is motivating in and of itself because it provides a sense of agency and autonomy: both ingredients for motivation and self-determination.
author: Rachael Gabriel
work: Reading's Non-Negotiables
expand: Since reading is an interaction between a reader and a text within a given context, giving a choice of texts allows readers to create the best possible interaction for the greatest possible level of engagement. Choice is also motivating in and of itself because it provides a sense of agency and autonomy: both ingredients for motivation and self-determination.

quote: Learning is driven by clear distinctions — knowing what something is requires knowing what it isn't. (Carl Hendrick, as quoted in Clark's piece)
author: Craig Barton
work: 3-Read Friday #112: Non-examples, screen time, and better evidence for teachers
expand: Craig Barton's weekly digest curates three blog posts for educators. The first, by Jamie Clark, explores non-examples—deliberately chosen contrast cases that share surface features with a concept but lack its critical defining feature—and argues they are essential to forming accurate conceptual schema in students' long-term memory. The second, by Dan Meyer, challenges blunt calls to ban screens in schools, arguing that screens are most valuable when woven briefly and purposefully alongside talk, paper, and peers rather than dominating a lesson. The third, by Jim Hewitt and Nidhi Sachdeva, introduces 'The Evidence Checker,' an AI-powered tool that gives teachers direct access to educational research so they can evaluate classroom claims themselves rather than relying on intermediaries. Barton frames all three as contributions to the same underlying project: building clearer, more disciplined, more evidence-grounded teaching practice.

quote: Once you have learned how to ask questions, relevant and appropriate and substantial questions, you have learned how to learn, and no one can keep you from learning whatever you want or need to know.
author: Neil Postman
work: Teaching as a Subversive Activity
expand: Knowledge is produced in response to questions. And new knowledge results from the asking of new questions, quite often new questions about old questions. Once you have learned how to ask questions, relevant and appropriate and substantial questions, you have learned how to learn and no one can keep you from learning whatever you want or need to know.

quote: The more I read about how large language models work, the more I realise they are hungry for that kind of context. The flowery asides we filter out when typing turn out to be exactly what sharpens the output.
author: Craig Barton
work: How I Use AI as a Thought Partner
expand: Craig Barton, a math education expert, presents a five-step framework for using AI as a genuine thought partner in instructional design rather than as a cognitive outsourcing machine or a simple formatter. He distinguishes two traps: the first is cognitive outsourcing (letting AI do the thinking while you copy and paste), and the second is under-using AI (doing all the thinking yourself and using AI only for layout). His framework occupies the productive middle: end every prompt with 'Before you build, ask me some clarifying questions'; invite the AI to be a critical collaborator that challenges your thinking; scrutinize the first output like a colleague's draft; iterate through multiple versions; and ask the AI what it learned from the collaboration at the end. Barton illustrates the framework with five concrete examples from his recent work designing sequences of positive and negative examples for teaching categorical concepts in mathematics, showing how AI pushed back on his proposed examples and improved the sequence. He also argues that voice input via tools like Wispr Flow frees working memory to focus on ideas rather than typing, and that the context-rich, discursive prompts enabled by dictation produce better AI outputs.

quote: Algebra instruction should move students beyond superficial mathematics knowledge and toward a deeper understanding, encouraging students to make connections between algebraic concepts and the procedures present in problems.
author: IES What Works Clearinghouse
work: Teaching Strategies for Improving Algebra Knowledge in Middle and High School Students
expand: Although proficiency in arithmetic operations is important to becoming proficient in algebra, the recommendations advocate algebra instruction that moves students beyond superficial mathematics knowledge and toward a deeper understanding of algebra. This includes encouraging students to make connections between algebraic concepts and the procedures present in problems.

quote: Block practice tells them which skill to use. Interleaving makes them choose. Mixing topics forces students to do the thing exams demand and classrooms rarely rehearse: working out which method a question is even asking for.
author: Craig Barton
work: Introducing The Ultimate Retrieval Tool
expand: Barton announces The Ultimate Retrieval Tool, a free mathematics quiz generator designed to address six specific pedagogical problems that he argues no existing tool solves: quiz regeneration via a shareable code, fixed 4- or 10-question formats that fit exactly on one side of A4, blocked or interleaved topic selection, geometry diagram generation, per-question regeneration with a single click, and follow-up quiz generation across a whole quiz. The article is structured as a rationale for each of the six design decisions, explaining the pedagogical reasoning behind choices like limiting to 4 or 10 questions (4 for a retrieval Do Now, 10 for a weekly low-stakes quiz), so readers understand not just what the tool does but why. A persistent frustration Barton addresses is the problem of quiz ephemerality: tools that generate good quizzes cannot reproduce them, making it impossible to project answers after printing, share a quiz with a colleague, or offer students a homework version of the same content. The interleaving feature reflects decades of research on desirable difficulties: mixed-topic quizzes force students to determine which method a question requires, not just execute a known procedure — which is what examinations demand but what most classroom practice never rehearses. The geometry diagram capability represents a significant technical advance: AI can now render diagrams reliably, expanding retrieval practice beyond arithmetic and algebra to angle facts, statistical diagrams, trigonometry, and circle theorems. Four practical use cases are described: the mixed-topic Do Now, the weekly low-stakes quiz, the in-lesson follow-up using the regeneration button after explanation, and student self-quizzing at home using the shareable code.

quote: Having children read to themselves is the foundation for creating independent readers and writers.
author: Gail Boushey
work: The Daily Five
expand: Having children read to themselves is the first step in Daily Five and is the foundation for creating independent readers and writers. On the surface, Read to Self seems basic and simple. However, we have found that specific teaching techniques can make Read to Self a powerful tool for enhancing all literacy skills.

quote: The findings underscore the importance of student behaviors and the teacher-student relationship in shaping how teachers perceive their students’ well-being.
author: Craig Barton (summarising Zurbriggen et al.)
work: Research Bite 49 Teacher Judgment Accuracy of Student Well-Being
expand: Teachers' judgments of student well-being (life satisfaction, emotions, social belonging) are surprisingly inaccurate, particularly for students in minority groups and lower academic achievers; teacher predictions explain only 10-25 percent of variance in student well-being scores, with pronounced blind spots for students who mask distress or who differ from the teacher in culture or identity. Schools increasingly rely on teacher observation to screen for mental health needs and allocate support, yet observation alone misses half the students who need help and flags many who do not. Combining teacher judgment with simple student self-report surveys significantly improves detection.

quote: The goal is always to ensure that students are active participants in the learning, not passive recipients.
author: Jeff C. Marshall
work: The Highly Effective Teacher
expand: All students need to be confident communicators and thoughtful writers, so we need to help them learn to grow their strengths and improve their weaknesses. We can achieve this using a variety of strategies, but the goal is always to ensure that students are active participants in the learning, not passive recipients.

quote: To treat this ballooning group of students as a differentiation or 'individual needs' issue is a fool's errand that will inevitably lead to the burnout of the profession.
author: Rebecca Birch
work: On What We Mean When We Say Inclusive Teaching
expand: This article challenges the prevailing understanding of inclusive teaching, arguing that the dominant interpretation — which emphasises catering to individual uniqueness and diagnostic categories — is counterproductive and ultimately insulting to students with disabilities. Rebecca Birch, an Australian educator, points out that 27% of Australian students now have a disability and that systemic incentives around the NDIS and school funding have driven diagnostic inflation to the point where treating diverse learners as an 'individual needs' problem has become a practical impossibility. Her alternative: true inclusion means recognising that students with diverse learning profiles share the same fundamental cognitive architecture as everyone else, and therefore benefit from the same evidence-based practices — priming, clear explanations, predictability, safety, and guided practice — that benefit all learners. The article takes particular aim at personalised learning plans that specify obvious adjustments as though they were accommodations, arguing that the real barrier to inclusion is not lack of individualisation but lack of explicit, structured, high-quality teaching for everyone.

quote: The sensory system acts more like a sieve than a sponge. Because we are bombarded with multisensory input at any given time, we are selective about what we give attention.
author: Gayle Gregory & Martha Kaufeldt
work: The Motivated Brain
expand: The sensory system acts more like a sieve than a sponge. Because we are bombarded with multisensory input at any given time, we are selective about what we give attention. In fact, we do not respond to 90 percent of stimuli available. Novel or interesting or threatening stimuli are more likely to garner our attention.

quote: Expertise goes global when educators can connect across borders in real time.
author: Rebecca Birch
work: Season Two Episode 2 Expertise Goes Global
expand: Educational expertise is increasingly global as educators connect across borders through platforms like podcasts and online communities. The episode explores how expertise in teaching and pedagogy transcends national boundaries and how teachers worldwide share learning science insights. This globalization of education creates opportunities for teachers everywhere to access world-class pedagogical thinking and participate in international conversations about student learning.

quote: Learning is the constant; time becomes the variable. Students stay with the learning long enough to be proficient or advanced.
author: Copper Stoll & Gene Giddings
work: Reawakening the Learner
expand: Gone are the days when learners complete a unit of study, receive their grades, and move on to the next unit whether they've mastered the material early or needed more time. In a learner-centric classroom, students stay with the learning long enough to be proficient or advanced. That means time becomes the variable; learning is the constant.

quote: Relying on students working at home is, however, a risky business. Even once students are in good habits around completing the homework, we can always question whether they have actually learnt anything.
author: Adam Boxer
source: Carousel Teaching, Learning, Leading Substack, December 22, 2025
work: The Greatest Do Now Ever
expand: Chronicles how an early-career science teacher named Tom pushed back on departmental Do Now practices, sparking a complete redesign of classroom retrieval and accountability systems. The new system integrates homework flashcards, self-assessment quizzes, in-class quiz checks, and consequences for failing to meet mastery thresholds. Students must prove learning on homework through in-class quizzes tied directly to the same content; cheating becomes nearly impossible due to triangulation between online homework, written exercise book evidence, and class assessment. Low performers are identified quickly and remediated; high performers get success experiences. The system is deceptively simple in execution but complex in thought: it closes the accountability gap between homework and achievement while keeping workload manageable. The author, initially defensive of existing practices, was convinced by the evidence and is now implementing it across his own classes with strong results.

quote: Games bring fun and energy into a learning zone by engaging players, motivating them to interact with the topic and linking positive emotions to the learning process.
author: Rob Plevin
work: The Fun Teacher's Tool Kit
expand: Reason #1: Games Make learning Fun. Games bring fun and energy into a learning zone by engaging players, motivating them to interact with the topic and linking positive emotions to the learning process. Learners are able to demonstrate their understanding of a topic in a friendly contest where successes are memorable moments of shared triumph and celebration and where mistakes are not seen as a personal weakness.

quote: Routines are the building blocks of the classroom culture. They are not merely administrative tasks; they are instructional infrastructure.
author: Brendan
work: The Architecture of Attention Why Routines Are Prerequisites for Learning
expand: Brendan argues that classroom routines—not content—form the invisible instructional infrastructure necessary for student learning. By explicitly teaching procedures through a five-step model (plan, teach, practice, provide feedback, revise), teachers reduce cognitive load and create predictable environments where students focus on learning rather than logistics. The article uses mini-whiteboards as a case study in standardizing even mundane classroom workflows, with practical scripts for teachers.

quote: These mathematical routines are structured activities that, when used consistently, can help students gain proficiency with a range of concepts and practices.
author: Yeild Math Routines - Unknown
work: High-Yield Routines
expand: Many textbooks make suggestions and give directions for a variety of mathematical routines. These mathematical routines are structured activities that, when used consistently, can help students gain proficiency with a range of concepts and practices. Some of these routines are well known and commonly implemented.

quote: Because of this, small details are not small at all. They are the mechanisms through which learning either becomes possible or quietly slips away.
author: Laura Burke
work: Sweating the Small Stuff
expand: This article by a former headteacher argues that the difference between a good classroom and a great one is found not in dramatic pedagogical strategies or bold approaches but in hundreds of tiny acts of deliberate attention that compound over time. The author describes her signature leadership phrase — 'we sweat the small stuff' — as shorthand for a way of noticing and caring about classroom details that rarely appear in policies or improvement plans: which paper to model on, the pause before a question, the choice of which student to call on first and why, circulating with an intentional route. Burke argues that great teachers pay extraordinary attention to the ordinary because they understand that learning is fragile, attention is limited, and working memory is easily overwhelmed — making the specific details of what students look at, listen to, and think about at each moment critical to whether learning becomes possible or quietly slips away. The piece concludes that these habits compound across lessons and years, making incremental invisible improvements that reshape students' experience of learning in ways that grand strategies never can.

quote: What actually happens is that one or two students discover exactly what I wanted them to discover. And the rest of the students do not have a flipping clue what is going on.
author: Craig Barton
work: How I Wish I'd Taught Maths
expand: What actually happens is that one or two students discover exactly what I wanted them to discover. A handful of students have some kind of idea what is going on, but with an eclectic mix of gaps in their knowledge and newly formed misconceptions. And the rest of the students do not have a flipping clue what is going on. They are feeling confused and pretty down about themselves when they see their fellow classmates have figured it out.

quote: Digital prostheses don't just complete tasks, they pilfer our students' chances to exercise the capacities they need to develop.
author: Andrew Cantarutti
work: The Right Tool for the Right Hands
expand: Andrew Cantarutti argues that schools systematically misapply EdTech evaluation frameworks by using the same efficiency standard for teacher-facing and student-facing tools -- a standard that makes sense for the former but fires badly when applied to the latter. His core distinction: teacher-facing tools should be evaluated for professional utility (do they free the teacher to do more of the work that requires human judgment?), while student-facing tools must be evaluated for developmental cost (what cognitive capacity does this tool displace that the student is still building?). Unlike teachers, who are formed professionals, students are in the middle of building the cognitive architecture that underpins everything else. The essay's key insight is that some tools -- particularly Learning Management Systems like Google Classroom -- are sold to teachers on efficiency grounds but function by routing student behavior through the platform, offloading executive function that students need to develop. The article provides a two-tier framework and practical talking points for making the case to administrators.

quote: If he fills his allotted time with drilling his students in routine operations he kills their interest, hampers their intellectual development, and misuses his opportunity.
author: Jonathan D. Katz
work: Developing Mathematical Thinking: A Guide to Rethinking the Mathematics Classroom
expand: Polya (1945) once said: Thus, a teacher of mathematics has a great opportunity. If he fills his allotted time with drilling his students in routine operations he kills their interest, hampers their intellectual development, and misuses his opportunity. But if he challenges the curiosity of his students by setting their problems proportionate to their knowledge, and helps them to solve their problems with stimulating questions, he may give them a taste for, and some means of, independent thinking.

quote: Teachers know and understand this and many were waving their hands to call attention to these issues far before many of their system leaders responsible for purchasing decisions were taking notice.
author: Caracappa, Michele
work: Against Curriculum Dilution
expand: Caracappa draws on a March 2026 Kappan piece and a RAND study to examine why teachers routinely reduce the rigor of high-quality instructional materials (HQIM) -- a phenomenon researchers call curriculum dilution. She distinguishes between dilution (teachers simplifying tasks because they doubt students can handle them) and the legitimate critique that even 'all-green' EdReports curricula have real quality gaps. The research from Jackson et al. shows that teachers who maintained high expectations found ways to support students without reducing cognitive demand, while those who doubted students lowered the bar. Caracappa argues for curriculum fortification: a dual commitment to developing teachers' capacity to implement materials with fidelity and to openly improving the curriculum itself where it falls short. She proposes collaborative professional learning structures -- student-work protocols, inclusive professional learning with specialists, and collective sense-making -- as the institutional moves that build both teacher efficacy and student access to grade-level content.

quote: A good essential question is open-ended; that is, it typically will not have a single, final, and correct answer.
author: Jay McTighe
work: Essential Questions: Opening Doors to Student Understanding
expand: Here are seven defining characteristics. A good essential question is open-ended; that is, it typically will not have a single, final, and correct answer. It is thought-provoking and intellectually engaging, often sparking discussion and debate. It calls for higher-order thinking, such as analysis, inference, evaluation, prediction. It cannot be effectively answered by recall alone.

quote: The relationship between coach and coachee is central to success in instructional coaching.
author: Carousel Teaching
work: Questioning Coaching
expand: Instructional coaching is well-evidenced in research but faces substantial implementation challenges that schools often underestimate. The core problems are psychological, structural, and practical: creating a two-tier teacher system generates discomfort and status anxiety; the term 'instructional coaching' lacks consistent definition across training providers, resulting in incoherent programs; implementation demands expert observers and significant budgets; and there is no consensus on what constitutes good teaching. Teachers elevated to coaching roles may be excellent practitioners yet lack coaching skill—two very different competencies. The research shows that the most effective coaching approach differs markedly depending on the target teacher's expertise level, yet schools rarely differentiate. Before committing to instructional coaching, schools must honestly assess whether it fits their culture, capacity, and current needs. Rushing into the model without addressing foundational questions leads to wasted investment and staff demoralization.

quote: Its most important and dominant characteristic is that it informs the student, the teacher, and all other interested parties about how to best enhance student learning.
author: Robert J. Marzano
work: Formative Assessment and Standards-Based Grading
expand: Feedback can be given formally or informally in group or one-on-one settings. It can take a variety of forms. As the preceding definitions illustrate, its most important and dominant characteristic is that it informs the student, the teacher, and all other interested parties about how to best enhance student learning.

quote: Leaders tell me that they think the policy is good and that teachers are following it, but student outcomes don't seem to be improving, and they aren't sure why.
author: Carousel: Teaching, Learning, Leading
work: Long-Term Memory Problem Why Teaching and Learning Policies Fail
expand: A school walkthrough reveals well-written policies, strong training, and visibly aligned practice, yet when asked three-week recall questions from exercise books, almost no student can answer. The school has in-the-moment teaching strategy but almost nothing for learning over time. Long-term retrieval fails systematically because historic curriculum discourse undervalued knowledge durability, observations reward easier signals, curriculum time feels too tight, teachers avoid the depression of testing recall, and seductive quick-fixes like Do Nows substitute for real spacing strategy. The load-bearing argument is stark: if curriculum has not been coded for retrieval, scheduled with proper spacing, and reinforced through high-quality homework, nothing done in class can compensate, and outcomes looking out of line with classroom quality usually trace to this missing layer.

quote: When you clearly define and communicate your expectations for transitions, you will have well-managed and efficient transitions.
author: Randy Sprick
work: CHAMPS: A Proactive and Positive Approach to Classroom Management
expand: One type of transition is when students move from one task to another during an activity. When you clearly define and communicate your expectations for transitions, you will have well-managed and efficient transitions. It is important to recognize that no two teachers have exactly the same set of expectations for their classrooms.

quote: When done well, schools still don't improve without systems to sustain implementation.
author: Carolyn Carousel
work: When Done Well Why Schools Dont Improve
expand: Educational improvement initiatives often fail despite good intentions because schools lack the coherent implementation infrastructure to sustain change. When reforms are well-designed but poorly implemented, they create the illusion of action without actual progress. The critical factor determining school improvement is not whether initiatives are theoretically sound, but whether schools have the capacity to execute them consistently across classrooms. This analysis distinguishes between having good ideas and having the organizational systems necessary to turn those ideas into sustained teaching practice.

quote: This isn't a book about how to teach Common Core; it's about good teaching practices. Standards, curricula, and educational trends come and go, but good teaching is timeless.
author: John Stevens & Matt Vaudrey
work: The Classroom Chef: Sharpen Your Lessons, Season Your Classes, Make Math Meaningful
expand: You'll notice as you read, however, that we don't talk about the Common Core State Standards. This isn't a book about how to teach Common Core; it's about good teaching practices. Standards, curricula, and educational trends come and go, but good teaching is timeless. Good teaching isn't focused solely on Common Core, nor on EdTech, and definitely not on the latest trend discussed at the staff meeting.

quote: Delivery is not the same as learning. Presentation is not the same as understanding. And the fact that content has been taught tells you almost nothing about whether it has been retained.
author: Jamie Lee Clark
work: Poor Proxies #5: The Finish Line Fallacy
expand: Jamie Lee Clark examines the Finish Line Fallacy: the widespread assumption that presenting content to students constitutes learning. Rob Coe's research identifies curriculum coverage as a poor proxy for learning because presentation, however clear, does not guarantee retention. Clark draws on Robert Bjork's work on desirable difficulties and Graham Nuthall's landmark research showing that students require 3-4 encounters with content in different contexts before reliable long-term retention occurs. She argues that schools often abandon students who didn't understand material the first time by moving forward based on coverage rather than verified learning. Clark provides five practical strategies to shift from coverage-based success measures to retention-based design, emphasizing spaced retrieval, learning checks without notes, multiple encounters across units, and daily review practices.

quote: In traditional mathematics education, we have assumed students understand the concepts of mathematics if they could perform the algorithms.
author: Jennifer Wathall
work: Concept-Based Mathematics: Teaching for Deep Understanding
expand: In traditional mathematics education, we have assumed students understand the concepts of mathematics if they could perform the algorithms. It was a step forward as we required students to explain their thinking on mathematical problems, but this still did not ensure that students really understood the conceptual relationships inherent in the problem.

quote: Hands-up question and answer is a sampling strategy — and it's a deeply biased one. The students most likely to raise their hand are those who already know the answer. The students most likely to stay silent are those who don't.
author: Jamie Lee Clark
work: Poor Proxies 6 The Few vs The Many
expand: Jamie Lee Clark closes the six-part Poor Proxies for Learning series by examining the most pervasive proxy: the assumption that a few students' correct answers represent whole-class understanding. Drawing on Dylan Wiliam's formative assessment research, the article argues that hands-up question and answer is a structurally biased sampling strategy — it selects precisely the students already confident in the material, rendering the students most in need of instructional adjustment invisible. Research by Fortney, Johnson, and Long (2001) shows that 'compulsive communicators' in hands-up classrooms don't merely consume airtime but actively suppress participation development in surrounding students, producing measurably smaller gains in communication competence. The article argues that the critical moment requiring whole-class evidence is the transition point — before moving from guided to independent practice, or from one concept to the next — where instructional decisions must be based on class-wide data rather than confidence-filtered volunteer responses. Clark presents five simultaneous whole-class response strategies — mini-whiteboards, cold calling, hinge questions, exit tickets, and think-pair-share — each designed to surface diagnostic evidence from every student in seconds rather than confirming what the confident few already know.

quote: The genius of rubrics is that they are descriptive and not evaluative. The operating principle is you match the performance to the description rather than judge it.
author: Susan M. Brookhart
work: How to Create and Use Rubrics for Formative Assessment and Grading
expand: Thus rubrics are as good or bad as the criteria selected and the descriptions of the levels of performance under each. Effective rubrics have appropriate criteria and well-written descriptions of performance.

quote: Learning is driven by clear distinctions — knowing what something is requires knowing what it isn't.
author: Jamie Clark
work: Non-Examples: How Showing What Something ISN'T Sharpens What IT IS
expand: Jamie Clark provides a comprehensive, evidence-grounded guide to non-examples—deliberately chosen contrast cases that share surface features with a concept but lack its critical defining feature—arguing they are essential tools for forming accurate conceptual schema rather than optional additions to teaching. Drawing on Archer and Hughes' Explicit Instruction (2011), Engelmann and Carnine (1982), and Rosenshine's Principles of Instruction, Clark explains that examples alone create either too-narrow or too-broad schema; non-examples define the boundary between what something is and what it is not. The piece systematically distinguishes non-examples from random wrong answers, trick questions, and misconceptions, and provides a five-step implementation framework: identify the critical feature, choose non-examples that share surface features, sequence examples and non-examples consecutively, include boundary examples, and use pause-and-reflect routines. The pathetic fallacy illustration—showing how students who learn pathetic fallacy from examples alone begin labeling every weather reference as pathetic fallacy—demonstrates the real cost of omitting non-examples from instruction.

quote: Feedback about the qualities of the work and feedback about the process or strategies used to do the work are most helpful.
author: Susan M. Brookhart
work: How to Give Effective Feedback to Your Students
expand: The level at which the feedback is focused influences its effectiveness. Feedback that draws students' attention to their self-regulation strategies can be effective. Personal comments like 'Good girl!' do not draw students' attention to their learning.

quote: Results like these aren't normal; my teachers are, for lack of a better word, weird—weird in a way every school should want.
author: Science of Reading Classroom
source: Science of Reading Classroom Substack, January 26, 2026
work: A Blueprint for Weird Teachers, Schools, and Districts
expand: Effective teachers share intense curiosity that drives continuous tinkering with instruction and content, constant learning despite success, and seeking connections with like-minded peers. School leaders cultivate curiosity by allocating regular paid time for discussing instructional practice, maintaining focus on pedagogy, structuring conversations around shared texts, building explicit experimentation culture, and identifying high-performing teachers to give them agency to lead others.

quote: At its most basic level, differentiating instruction means "shaking up" what goes on in the classroom so that students have multiple options for taking in information, making sense of ideas, and expressing what they learn.
author: Carol Ann Tomlinson
work: How to Differentiate Instruction in Mixed-Ability Classrooms
expand: In a differentiated classroom, commonalities are acknowledged and built upon, and student differences also become important elements in teaching and learning. At its most basic level, differentiating instruction means "shaking up" what goes on in the classroom so that students have multiple options for taking in information, making sense of ideas, and expressing what they learn.

quote: Check my modelling is where the coach puts the coachee in the position of the students and gets them to check that the model does what it needs to do.
author: Cognitive Coaching (Adam Kohlbeck)
work: Check My Modelling
expand: A short entry in the Coaching Cuts series, built around a video of coach Adam working with coachee Katie. The technique has a clean name: check my modelling. The coach runs the model themselves, and the coachee watches from the student's seat, checking that the model actually delivers what a student needs to take away. The frame flips the usual coaching gaze. Instead of the coachee watching for technique, they are watching for landing. The prerequisites matter and the piece is explicit about them. Before the model runs, coach and coachee agree on what the students must take away, and they codify it as a simple success criterion. Adam models a modelling task (he is drawing a model while modelling drawing a model, which the author cheerfully flags), narrates his thinking, and afterwards asks Katie, 'How close was I to the success criteria for the kids?' That question is the hinge. It forces the coachee to evaluate against a shared external standard rather than a generic impression of clarity. The why is the piece's strongest turn for primary-classroom use. Models only work if they land. Teachers routinely assume their modelling was clear because it was clear to them, and miss that students lost the key moves, misread the reasoning, or got absorbed in surface detail. Running the check against an agreed criterion surfaces those blind spots in a way post-hoc observation rarely does. The cautions are equally useful: do not run this technique when the coachee is already cognitively overloaded, and do not run it without a shared definition of good, or the analysis drifts into opinion.

quote: When students feel safe taking risks and making mistakes, then they will, with proper guidance, challenge themselves in amazing ways.
author: John Joseph
work: The Highly Effective Teacher: 7 Classroom-Tested Practices for Student Success
expand: When students feel safe taking risks and making mistakes, then they will, with proper guidance, challenge themselves in amazing ways. If, however, students fear failing, they will often avoid even trying so that they don't look dumb in front of their peers. If participation from all students is the goal, then the culture needs to provide a safe and challenging yet low-stress environment.

quote: The most important single factor influencing learning is what the learner already knows. Ascertain this and teach him accordingly.
author: Cognitive Coaching
work: The Prior Knowledge Paradox, What to Do with What They Know
expand: Despite Ausubel's famous principle that prior knowledge is the most important factor in learning, recent research complicates this assumption. A meta-analysis found prior knowledge shows near-zero correlation with learning gains, and an RCT found background knowledge did not improve learning of new domain material. The critical issue lies in varying definitions, mediators, and moderators affecting how prior knowledge influences learning. Ausubel's advance organisers scaffold and constrain prior knowledge before new instruction, making them more effective than relying on existing familiarity.

quote: The brain is naturally curious, and offering puzzles or conflicting or discrepant events will cause the brain to begin SEEKING answers or solutions.
author: Gayle Gregory & Martha Kaufeldt
work: The Motivated Brain: Improving Student Attention, Engagement, and Perseverance
expand: Curiosity. The brain is naturally curious, and offering puzzles or conflicting or discrepant events will cause the brain to begin SEEKING answers or solutions. A principal was observing a student teacher preparing to teach a science lesson about matter; the mystery she created had the children's rapt attention.

quote: Our students join us at college somewhere on the road between safe, transactional approaches to their work and the curiosity and risk required to become knowledge creators.
author: Curious Pedagogy (Jennifer & George)
work: The Unknown Can Be a Scary Space
expand: Written from a college instructor's perspective, this piece uses the Scaredy Squirrel children's book as an extended metaphor to explore why students fear venturing into the unknown and how instructors can help them develop strategies to do so. The authors trace their own journeys from risk-averse students to scholars and use this personal context to make the case for epistemic humility -- positioning oneself at the borders of uncertainty -- as a teachable, practicable skill. The piece examines the tension students feel between the value of learning by failing and the real pressures of a grading system, and offers several pedagogical strategies: scaffolding, transparent learning goals, timely feedback, and metacognitive reflection. The authors address AI as a double-edged tool -- a potential risk-avoidance shortcut for transactional students, but a legitimate support for genuine scholarly exploration when used critically. The article ultimately argues that curiosity and willingness to explore the unknown are not innate traits but learnable stances that instructors must deliberately cultivate.

quote: Students can't learn if they feel scared, desperately alone, invisible, unvalued, unknown. If students feel cared for, valued, and important, they will feel safe enough to learn and grow.
author: Kim Campbell
work: If You Can't Manage Them, You Can't Teach Them
expand: If students don't like you or don't feel you like them, they are not going to like being in your class, and they are not going to work hard. Students can't learn if they feel scared, desperately alone, invisible, unvalued, unknown. If students feel cared for, valued, and important—if they feel you are genuinely interested in them—they will feel safe enough to learn and grow.

quote: From this moment onward, you're not just a student; you're also a scholar.
author: George Cusack
work: Students vs. Scholars
expand: George Cusack, a professor at Carleton University, reframes first-year students as 'scholars' from day one -- active knowledge contributors rather than passive knowledge recipients. He argues that students locked in a purely transactional mentality focus exclusively on grade optimization, avoiding genuine intellectual risk-taking and the curiosity that productive academic work requires. This transactional framing also distorts how students engage with AI: they treat it as a grade-efficiency tool rather than as an object of critical inquiry. Students who adopt a scholar identity become curious about AI's capabilities and limitations, willing to share discoveries with peers, and more capable of evaluating AI as scholars rather than as consumers. Cusack offers a concrete assignment -- having student-scholars test AI capabilities and report findings -- as a practical entry point for building genuine AI literacy in any discipline. Key Points * A 'student' receives knowledge passively; a 'scholar' actively seeks to add something new -- Cusack places this distinction at the front of every syllabus. * The transactional mentality steers students toward the path of least resistance: familiar concepts, avoided risk, and work that no one genuinely cares about. * Students in transactional mode approach AI strategically (avoiding honor code risks, cutting workload), whereas scholar-identified students approach AI with curiosity about its limits. * Creating classroom space for sharing AI experiments builds a genuine community of inquiry and shifts how students relate to both research and technology. * The scholar reframe shifts academic work from grade extraction to genuine contribution to a shared knowledge community.

quote: Here is the First Great Law of Whole Brain Teaching: The longer we talk, the more students we lose.
author: Chris Biffle
work: Whole Brain Teaching for Challenging Kids
expand: So, I saw what many of us in education know instinctively; there is something dreadfully wrong with the lecture model. Our kids listen, maybe they take notes, but soon, they glaze over, zone out, are lost inside private worlds. And it doesn't matter if the students are in college or kindergarten. Here is the First Great Law of Whole Brain Teaching: The longer we talk, the more students we lose.

quote: When students are new to a domain, problem solving extracts a heavy toll on working memory.
author: Didau, David
work: Five Principles of Effective Modelling
expand: Modeling is essential for teaching but often done poorly. Single exemplars don't work, overly polished models discourage students, and direct copying replaces understanding. Didau outlines five principles for effective modeling: comparison clarifies quality, model structure not content, surface problems before solutions, use narration to direct attention, and gradually release responsibility. Good models reduce cognitive load for novices but become counterproductive for growing experts (expertise reversal effect).

quote: Mathematics gradually became a mysterious entity, whose rules and steps I was expected to unquestioningly memorise.
author: Ed Southall
work: Yes, But Why? Teaching for Understanding in Mathematics
expand: Mathematics gradually became a mysterious entity, whose rules and steps I was expected to unquestioningly memorise, which I dutifully did. However, the process of storing numerous algorithms and their quirky properties became increasingly tedious, and I fell out of love with the subject that once intrigued and excited me.

quote: At its core, a knowledge-rich curriculum is not a claim about how much pupils know, but about what knowledge does. It begins from a view of thinking as something that depends on what is known.
author: David Didau
work: A Seat at the Table Knowledge Inheritance and Purpose of Schooling
expand: Didau's case for the knowledge-rich curriculum rescues the term from caricature (rote memorization, silent classrooms, stripped agency) by reframing it around evidence from cognitive science: knowledge is the precondition for every higher-order skill schools claim to value; you cannot think well about what you know nothing about. Curriculum is not a neutral technical exercise but a promise made to young people about what they will need to understand the world they are entering. Subjects are distinct intellectual traditions with their own criteria for explanation and evidence, and pupils are apprenticed into them through carefully chosen scholarly tasks. The democratic payoff is sharp: public discourse assumes historical, scientific, and literary reference; if only the already advantaged can follow it, schools have failed their civic function. The remaining gap is writing instruction, where knowledge-rich intent too often meets skills-poor enactment.

quote: Her terrible experiences in math class have very little to do with mathematics as it really is.
author: Tracy Zager
work: Becoming the Math Teacher You Wish You'd Had
expand: Mostly, I wanted her to learn the difference between mathematics and math class, because her terrible experiences in math class, at home and at school, have very little to do with mathematics as it really is. I began telling her about math as I've come to know it, including the creativity, curiosity, play, puzzling, and attention to detail essential to the field.

quote: Making five-year-olds squeal with delight by burping doesn't require all that much. Honestly, he'd have been hard pressed not to over-excite them. It struck me that doing things that are easy is, well, easy.
author: Didau, David
work: Easy Things Are Easy Hard Things Are Hard
expand: Didau contrasts two teaching styles using the metaphor of entertainers: Johnny G (charismatic, low-bar, excitement-focused) versus Janet (calm, focused on meaningful activity like sewing). While excitement is easy, creating focused learning environments where students work hard on important, difficult tasks requires considerable skill and organizational support. New teachers often default to entertainment to manage classroom behavior, but experience and school culture supporting discipline enable them to prioritize deep work. Didau argues that if schools value culturally rich curricula and student mastery, they must support teachers in maintaining order and valuing hard work rather than leaving it to chance.

quote: Eustress is the productive strain that accompanies deliberate practice. The feeling students get when they are required to retrieve information rather than recognise it, to explain ideas rather than copy them.
author: David Didau
work: From Distress to Eustress Why Some Stress Is Good for You
expand: The relationship between stress and performance follows an inverted U-curve: too little stress produces apathy and shallow engagement, while too much overwhelms working memory and attention. Learning happens optimally in the uncomfortable middle ground where eustress—productive strain accompanying deliberate practice—creates cognitive demand without overwhelm. Students experience eustress through retrieval practice, explaining ideas rather than copying them, and public commitment (writing answers on whiteboards knowing they'll be seen). This cognitive demand strengthens memory and understanding. Teachers must intentionally design lessons to create this optimal stress level through checking attention and understanding, cold calling with no opt-out, and routines that force deliberate effort. The challenge is moving beyond eliminating stress as an educational toxin to recognizing productive stress as essential to effective learning.

quote: Prior knowledge supports learning only when ideas genuinely interlock. It is the structure of the content, not the thematic label, that determines what counts as a prerequisite.
author: David Didau
work: Missing the Wood for the Trees
expand: Two studies claiming that domain-adjacent prior knowledge fails to support learning rest on a methodological mistake: they assume any increase in knowledge should produce gains on any chosen assessment, ignoring whether the assessment actually depends on the knowledge taught. Buchin and Mulligan taught acoustic, tactile, and chemical perception but tested color perception, which depends on foundational science schema most teens already hold. The Education Endowment Foundation's Word and World trial taught history and geography but measured progress on decoding and inference, which the content does not address. When the conceptual bridge between content and assessment is missing, null effects are guaranteed. Prior knowledge supports learning only when ideas genuinely interlock structurally, not when they are thematically adjacent.

quote: A phone-free school should begin with culture, not kit.
author: David Didau
work: Phone Bans Are a Good Thing
expand: David Didau argues that school phone bans are right policy but that lockable pouches—currently the most commercially visible implementation—may be the wrong tool, because the phone's mere physical proximity continues to occupy cognitive bandwidth even when locked. A 2026 NBER study on lockable pouch adoption found in-class phone use fell from 61% to 13%, but standardized test scores showed effects 'consistently close to zero,' with first-year increases in disciplinary incidents and slight wellbeing dips. Didau contextualizes this against Ward et al.'s 2017 'brain drain' research, which showed cognitive performance degrades based on smartphone proximity—not just active use—meaning pouches solve the behavioral problem while leaving the psychological problem intact. He identifies three cheaper alternatives ranked by effectiveness: phones left at home (culturally clearest), phones switched off in bags with immediate confiscation if seen or heard, and tutor-time collection with end-of-day return. His deeper argument is that pouches convert a cultural challenge into a procurement decision, with the vendor (Yondr) supplying data and framing that subtly colonizes public education policy through commercial convenience, and that the ideal phone ban is invisible—phones simply absent from students' attentional field rather than locked on their person all day.

quote: Science can help us understand what is. It is far less helpful when it comes to deciding what ought to be. Facts about the world do not contain values hidden inside them, waiting to be discovered.
author: David Didau
work: Professional Scepticism
expand: Didau argues that teaching is peculiarly vulnerable to bad ideas not because teachers are credulous but because human reasoning is systematically biased and experience alone cannot reliably detect whether a practice works. Professional scepticism is the disciplined habit of questioning claims: what evidence supports them, how was the evidence generated, what would count against the claim? Science can help test claims about learning once aims are agreed, but it cannot settle by itself what schooling is for. Questions about purpose (transmission, employability, civic formation, flourishing) are moral and political. Didau's practical closing is a checklist for leaders: can we define the problem, what evidence suggests the intervention will work, how will we know if it works, when should we expect improvement, what if goals are not met, what will teachers do less of, are we respecting teacher expertise? The list is modest but serious, protecting time and trust while reframing scepticism as an ethical obligation.

quote: Institutions direct individual memory and channel our perceptions into forms compatible with the relations they authorize. Once a category becomes institutionally useful, evidence is interpreted in ways that reinforce it.
author: David Didau
work: Seeing Like a School Categories, Maps, Streets and Winks
expand: Drawing on institutional sociology and cognitive science, this essay examines how schools necessarily simplify complex daily reality to make institutions manageable. Yet these simplifications—categories like 'low ability' or labels for struggling teachers—can harden into self-fulfilling prophecies. Institutions don't merely use categories; they think with them, shaping what gets noticed and what gets ignored. The author synthesizes work by anthropologist Mary Douglas on institutional memory, psychologist Albert Michotte on causal inference, and political scientist James C.
