Scott on administrative legibility to argue that schools often mistake the proxies they create (grades, percentages, behavior codes) for the reality those proxies represent. Educators need methods for thinking beyond institutionalized categories to see both the mechanisms that drive problems and the possibilities for change.

quote: If we want better teachers we need better schools. And if we want better schools, we need better school leaders.
author: David Didau
work: The Coaching Trap: Why Better Teaching Depends on Better Schools
expand: David Didau critiques instructional coaching as a school improvement strategy, arguing that while coaching has real evidence behind it (Kraft et al's meta-analysis of 60 studies found positive effects on both instructional practice and student achievement), those effects shrink dramatically as programs scale. The root problem, Didau argues, is that schools take the structural form of coaching—the cycles, coaches, target-setting, forms—and assume they've captured the thing itself, when they've actually built monitoring with a thin veneer of support. Effective coaching, he insists, is a relationship organized around expertise: remove the expertise and you have therapy; remove the relationship and you have surveillance. More fundamentally, coaching is limited by the school environment it operates in. A teacher can be coached to improve questioning technique, but an unreliable behavior system blunts that improvement. Research shows teachers in schools at the 75th percentile for professional environment ratings improve 38% more over ten years than those at the 25th percentile—a larger effect than coaching alone. Didau concludes that school improvement must shift from an individualistic model (fix teachers one at a time) to a systemic one (build the conditions in which teachers improve naturally), and that this requires leaders who prioritize order, purposeful collaboration, instructionally credible leadership, high trust, and coherent culture.

quote: Personal effectiveness may be, at least in part, a relationship between our expertise and the conditions in which we work.
author: David Didau
work: The Myth of the Portable Teacher
expand: David Didau uses the Kraft and Papay Talent Transfer Initiative study to challenge the widespread belief that effective teachers carry their effectiveness wherever they go. The study, a randomized experiment offering high-performing teachers $20,000 to transfer to low-achieving schools, found that transfer teachers fell from the 85th to the 66th percentile of effectiveness after moving -- a decline of 0.12 student standard deviations. Didau argues that teacher effectiveness is not a stable, portable property but a relationship between individual expertise and institutional conditions. The essay extends the argument to school leadership, critiquing 'superhead' mythology, and proposes a surplus model of school leadership that assumes good faith and addresses systemic barriers rather than blaming individuals. The core claim is that teaching quality is produced by teachers working within particular environments, and improvement strategies must address the system, not just the teacher.

quote: A behaviour log is not an accurate transcript of school life.
author: David Didau
work: The Problem with Patterns: How Good Data Leads to Bad Decisions
expand: David Didau explains collider bias -- a statistical mechanism that generates false relationships in selected data -- through the lens of school behavior management. A headteacher's behavior lead found incident log clusters around specific corridors, times, and teachers, but the log is not a transcript of behavior -- it is a record of what was noticed, challenged, escalated, and written down. Teachers who trust the system and follow policy log more; those who don't trust escalation stop logging. When attention shifted to high-logging teachers, logging behavior changed and incident numbers fell -- not because behavior improved but because the metric was being managed. Collider bias (formalized by Berkson in 1946, developed through causal diagrams) arises when two causes share a common effect: conditioning on that effect creates false correlations between the causes. Examples: hospital samples make unrelated diseases appear inversely related (Berkson's paradox); university admissions make ability and wealth appear to trade off; dating apps make attractive people seem boring; Hollywood makes beautiful actors seem talentless; business studies of top salespeople who ignore the script mistake survivorship for genius. Schools are systematically vulnerable: every selected group they study (excluded students, persistently absent students, students in intervention) is the product of a selection process they then forget. Key Points * A behaviour log is not a transcript of behavior -- it is a record of what was noticed, challenged, escalated, and written down; teachers who trust the system log more, those who don't trust escalation log less, creating a collider that makes high-compliance teachers appear to be a behavior problem. * Collider bias (Berkson, 1946): when two causes share a common effect, conditioning on that effect creates spurious correlations between the causes that would not exist in the general population. * The mechanism appears across domains: hospital populations (Berkson's paradox), university admissions (ability/wealth tradeoff), dating apps (attractive/interesting tradeoff), Hollywood (beautiful/talented tradeoff), business (successful rule-breakers look like geniuses). * Controlling for a confounder reduces bias; controlling for a collider creates it -- the distinction requires understanding the causal structure of the data, not just adding more variables to the analysis. * The professional lesson: the easy availability of data tempts schools to study spreadsheets instead of doing the hard work of visiting lessons, patrolling corridors, talking to teachers and students -- and asking which students are invisible, which policies are being followed versus worked around.

quote: The common error in progressive pedagogy is trying to teach a concept by turning the explanation into a mandatory, step-by-step procedure. I call this the 'proceduralisation' of concepts.
author: (Brendan)
work: Why the Conceptual Understanding Debate Is Arguing Over the Wrong Premise
expand: The debate between procedural fluency and conceptual understanding has stalled because neither side has a precise vocabulary for what a concept actually is. Drawing on Tiemann and Markle's taxonomy, the article defines a concept as a mental category with Critical Attributes (must-haves) and Variable Attributes (can-haves): concept learning has occurred only when a student can correctly classify a completely novel, unseen instance. This framework clarifies the most common instructional error—the proceduralization of concepts—which occurs when a concept-teaching tool like a bar model is turned into a mandatory drawing algorithm, teaching a new procedure rather than the underlying category. It also clarifies valid concept assessment: tests that ask students to respond to new instances, not reproduce familiar diagrams. The Instructional Hierarchy of Haring and Eaton provides the developmental sequence: concept acquisition must precede fluency, which precedes generalization; collapsing these stages produces the problems both sides of the debate diagnose. Procedural and conceptual understanding are genuinely different cognitive architectures requiring different instructional designs, and the Tiemann-Markle framework makes both teachable and assessable with precision. KEY TAKEAWAYS * A concept is a mental category defined by Critical Attributes (must-haves) and Variable Attributes (can-haves); concept learning occurs only when a student classifies novel, unseen instances correctly. * Proceduralizing concepts—turning a concept-teaching tool into a mandatory step-by-step routine—teaches a new procedure, not the underlying category. * Valid concept assessment asks students to respond to new instances, not define or reproduce familiar diagrams; the latter tests only memorized verbal algorithms. * Procedural fluency and conceptual understanding are different cognitive architectures requiring different instructional designs—conflating them is the source of most classroom confusion in this debate. * The Instructional Hierarchy requires acquisition to precede fluency, which precedes generalization—collapsing these stages produces exactly the problems both sides of the debate are trying to solve.

quote: Learning to read well is more like learning to play chess than checkers. I have learned how to play chess before— I know the rules of the game, the names of the pieces and what they can do. I cannot, by any means, play chess well.
author: (Cauthen, Colton)
work: They Didn't Teach You How To Read
expand: Literacy and reading are not the same thing. Most people are taught to decode symbols into meaning but not to read in the fuller sense, which involves progressive mastery across Mortimer Adler's four levels: Elementary, Inspectional, Analytical, and Syntopical. The article argues that treating reading as a binary skill—you either can or cannot—rather than a developable complex skill causes three predictable harms: people conclude they dislike reading when they are simply not yet skilled enough; they plateau because they believe there is nothing more to develop; and they mistake volume for depth, exemplified by listening to hundreds of books at 2.5x speed and calling it reading. Like chess or surgery, the skill of reading admits degrees of mastery that most people never approach—and most do not realize this. The good news is that recognizing reading as a cultivatable skill reverses all three harms, opening a path toward a richer reading life built through discipline, curiosity, and good practice. KEY TAKEAWAYS * Literacy (decoding symbols) and reading in the full analytical sense are different skills; most people are taught the former and never develop the latter. * Adler's four-level hierarchy—Elementary, Inspectional, Analytical, Syntopical—frames reading as a progressively complex skill at which most people plateau at level one without realizing it. * Treating reading as binary rather than developmental causes people to conclude they dislike it when they are simply not yet skilled enough to experience its rewards. * The volume-hype reading scene—hundreds of books per year at 2.5x speed—mistakes breadth for depth and passive consumption for active, analytical engagement. * Recognizing reading as a cultivatable skill reverses all three harms: reframes frustration as a growth stage, reveals the plateau, and redirects attention from volume to quality.

quote: Ancient Greece introduces us to the idea that schooling can prepare different groups to play different roles in society.
author: Matthew Evans
work: Making Sense of School
expand: This extract from Matthew Evans's book 'Making Sense of School' asks why the purpose of schools is so contested by working backward through a brief history of formal education. Evans argues that schools exist to educate children 'so that...' -- and that the impossibility of completing that sentence with a single agreed answer explains decades of curriculum and pedagogy wars. Tracing schooling from ancient Rome's communal gatherings through Greek leadership preparation, Spartan military discipline, Indian Vedic and Buddhist curricula, medieval charitable education, and Horace Mann's 19th-century American Common Schools, Evans shows that every era layered a new 'so that' onto the institution without discarding the old ones. Mann's Common Schools added the concepts of efficiency (low unit-cost at scale), equality (every child's right to education), and social engineering (all classes taught together) -- concepts still embedded in modern schooling and still in tension with each other. The piece's animating argument is that the contested purpose of schools is not an accident but the inevitable result of an institution asked to carry too many different social functions at once, each inherited from a different historical moment and none ever fully replaced.

quote: Most teachers are not struggling because they lack resilience or commitment.
author: Findlater, Sarah
work: A More Sustainable Way Forward What Teaching Could Feel Like Next
expand: Final post in the 16-part teaching wellbeing series. Teacher burnout is not about lacking resilience but about structurally unsustainable work. Sustainability means designing work humans can do well over time: bounded expectations, systems that reduce friction, protected recovery time. AI can help if used to reduce repetition and offload low-value tasks, not to accelerate pace. Real change requires system design and leadership modeling, not individual mindset shifts.

quote: Focusing on high stakes testing didn't achieve much, and most of what it did achieve was to damage school systems in numerous ways.
author: Peter Greene
work: Reformster Nostalgia and New Old Mistakes
expand: Education reformers are experiencing nostalgia for Bush-Obama era bipartisan consensus on testing, choice, and accountability. Mike Petrilli notes that reform fatigue resulted from two decades of push-back and public exhaustion with high-stakes testing. However, Greene argues reform failed not from fatigue but because it didn't work. High-stakes testing narrowed curriculum, damaged schools, and produced no academic gains while serving data-tech overlords and privatization advocates. True reform would ask how to make schools better, not how to inject market mechanisms. Including actual teachers in reform conversations would ground proposals in classroom reality.

quote: We don’t read whole books in school nearly as much as we used to, and that’s not an accident. It’s a design choice baked into the standards and testing regime we’ve built.
author: Peter Greene
work: Whole Books and Slow School
expand: Greene traces the decline of whole-book reading in American schools to structural forces: Common Core's skill-heavy frame and testing regimes that reward speed over depth. When reading is treated as a transferable skill operable on any text, short passages become cheaper and easier to align; the result is steady substitution of excerpts for books. Whole books uniquely allow characters, ideas, and language to develop, train sustained attention, and invite reader-response conversations that require shared imaginative worlds. Greene extends the case to a broader principle of slow school: mathematics benefits from time with hard problems, history from long primary sources, writing from drafts that mature across weeks. The call is to resist speed-maximising assumptions and protect the experiences that make educated people.

quote: Some teachers are architects, building knowledge structures component by component
author: Nick Hart
work: Architects and Integrators
expand: Teachers fall into two pedagogical orientations: architects who design comprehensive knowledge structures and integrators who weave knowledge across contexts. Both have pedagogical merit. Architects build systematic, sequenced understanding. Integrators connect learning to real-world contexts. Excellence requires recognizing both approaches and understanding when each serves students best.

quote: By choosing to collaborate in depth on some subjects, it is inevitable that other subjects will not be looked at together which makes it even more important to have clarity of expectations.
author: Hart, Nick
work: Collaborative Planning Meetings
expand: Hart outlines how collaborative planning meetings can improve teaching quality when focused on substance: subject content sequencing, pedagogy, misconceptions, task design, and adaptation. He warns against tokenistic sharing and emphasizes planning at least two weeks ahead to allow time for team feedback. Leadership teams must strategically choose which subjects to examine in depth based on data and teacher needs. Hart provides mental models for both planning for other teachers (clarity, slide notes, plenty of practice, advance sharing) and preparing to teach lessons planned by others (seeking clarification, providing feedback, rehearsing explanations).

quote: Team meetings are often treated as a logistical necessity: a place to share information, review actions or move through an agenda. In practice, this underestimates their role.
author: Nick Hart
work: Leadership Handbook Team Meetings
expand: This piece reframes team meetings not as logistical necessities but as primary mechanisms through which leadership actually happens in schools. Rather than viewing meetings as forums for sharing information and moving through agendas, the author argues they simultaneously accomplish six overlapping forms of work: establishing direction, achieving alignment across roles and timescales, constructing shared understanding of problems, enabling commitment to action, making decisions with clear accountability, and building psychological safety. Ineffective meetings actively undermine school culture by narrowing attention and fragmenting understanding; productive meetings increase the quality of attention across the organization. The framework shifts the leader's role toward shaping interactions that enable better conversations and decisions to emerge.

quote: Most teams use meetings for logistics and communication. Those are the lowest-leverage functions on the list. The highest-leverage functions — subject knowledge, pedagogy — are the ones almost nobody touches.
author: Nick Hart
work: Team Meetings: A Driver of School Improvement
expand: Nick Hart argues that well-structured team meetings are among the most powerful and underutilised tools for school improvement. Drawing on his experience as a school leader, Hart identifies nine distinct functions that effective team meetings serve: developing subject knowledge, providing pedagogical advice, refining planning, advancing strategic priorities, focusing on vulnerable children, debriefing decisions, standardising expectations, managing communication and logistics, and conducting quality assurance. Most school teams use meetings for only one or two of these functions — typically logistics and communication — while the high-leverage functions like subject knowledge development and pedagogical practice are rarely addressed. Hart argues that school improvement is a collective professional development problem, and team meetings are the infrastructure through which collective development happens — or fails to.

quote: Reform done poorly is often worse than no reform at all.
author: Frederick Hess
source: Education Next / Old School with Rick Hess
work: Five Lessons for School Reformers: 2026 Edition
expand: Frederick Hess, drawing on 35 years of observation from high school teacher to AEI fellow, warns the new generation of school reformers—energized by science-of-reading mandates, school choice expansion, and post-pandemic reckoning—not to repeat the mistakes of the Bush-Obama era. He identifies five lessons: don't mistake legislative wins for lasting victories (the real work follows), resist drinking the Kool-Aid (true believers dismiss legitimate criticism), listen with genuine purpose rather than performing engagement, treat money as a mixed blessing rather than a solution, and recognize that six-sevenths of reform is invisible to outsiders. Written with candor about past failures—Reading First, teacher evaluation, Common Core—he argues that 'reform done poorly is often worse than no reform at all' and that today's reformers face the additional challenge of polarized echo chambers and algorithm-driven social media that make escape from tribal thinking harder than ever.

quote: When you are constantly trying to learn new information, you are working at the absolute limit of your capacity. It takes a lot of mental effort to learn something brand-new every single day.
author: (Keller, Shari)
work: The Simple Strategy That Turns Summer Boredom into Independent Projects
expand: Cultural pressure to prevent the summer slide leads parents and teachers to push children into academic catch-up work, often producing the opposite of the intended effect. The article argues that learning requires oscillation between challenge and confidence: always operating at the cognitive edge is exhausting and counterproductive, and children need summer to develop genuine fluency with what they already know before building on it. The master carpenter analogy is illuminating: roughly 85% of expert performance involves mastered core skills executed with ease, with only 15% at the growth edge—and that challenge is satisfying precisely because of the competent foundation beneath it. The case of Jessie, a tenth-grader testing at fourth-grade level, confirms the principle: when given permission to work within her comfort zone for months through graphic novels, low-stakes math games, and unstructured time, her foundations solidified, her comfort zone expanded naturally, and she moved through subsequent academic material faster than expected. Summer is best used not for advancement but for fluency-building through project-based, self-directed work that applies existing skills in contexts children own. The prescription is simple: more unstructured time, project scaffolding without evaluation pressure, and the parental courage to step off the advancement treadmill. KEY TAKEAWAYS * Learning requires oscillation between challenge and confidence; always operating at the cognitive edge is exhausting and counterproductive for children and adults alike. * The master carpenter analogy: roughly 85% of expert work involves mastered core skills, with only 15% at the growth edge—that challenge is satisfying because of the competent foundation underneath. * Summer is best used for fluency-building and confidence through project-based, self-directed work that applies existing academic skills in contexts the child owns. * Unstructured time combined with simple project scaffolding is the sweet spot: enough structure to sustain engagement, enough freedom to make the work feel like an adventure. * Allowing a struggling student to work within her comfort zone for months solidified her foundations, expanded her comfort zone naturally, and accelerated subsequent academic progress beyond expectation.

quote: Fluency isn't a bridge. It's a red carpet — and how you walk it determines what you find at the end.
author: (Lippert, Samantha)
work: The Red Carpet Effect A New Way to See Reading Fluency
expand: Reading fluency is better understood as a red carpet than a bridge: the carpet (fluency) must be crossed with ease and presence so that cognitive attention is available for the press line (vocabulary and background knowledge) and ultimately the award (comprehension). A reader still decoding at conscious effort has no capacity left to work the press line—and when the press line is dense with unfamiliar vocabulary, reporters step onto the carpet itself, slowing the crossing. The article's central reframe is that struggling readers are not broken—they are undertraveled: they have not walked enough carpets to build the automaticity that frees cognitive space for meaning-making. Fluency is also venue-specific; a reader fluent in narrative fiction may feel lost in a science text because domain vocabulary and background knowledge constitute different reporters in a different venue. The intervention is not drilling a single text through repetition but varied repetition across many texts and genres—more carpets, not more practice on the same one. The Simple View of Reading and Scarborough's Rope are not replaced by this framework but made visible and navigable. KEY TAKEAWAYS * The red carpet reframes fluency: the carpet is fluency, the press line is vocabulary and background knowledge, the award is comprehension—but only for readers who cross fully present. * Struggling readers are not broken; they are undertraveled—they have not walked enough carpets to develop the automaticity that frees cognitive space for meaning. * Fluency is venue-specific: a reader fluent in one text type may struggle completely in another because domain vocabulary and background knowledge are different reporters at a different carpet. * Transferable fluency requires varied repetition across many text types and domains—not repeated reading of a single text. * Wide, varied reading is not optional enrichment—it is the mechanism by which fluency generalizes until a reader can walk into almost any room and find their footing.

quote: The problem is that this approach typically leads to the same outcome: a presentation, training session, or class that's ripe with information yet strangely light on impact.
author: Melissa Muth Martinez
work: Start With The End
expand: Backward design, introduced by Wiggins and McTighe, flips the traditional order of instructional planning from content-first to outcome-first. Instead of choosing materials and activities and then assessing them, you begin by defining what learners should actually be able to do, then specify evidence of that learning, and only then select content and experiences that move toward that outcome. The pattern breaks the accident of 'ripe-with-information-but-light-on-impact' training. Martinez walks through the four anchors: outcome (what they should do differently), evidence (how you'll know they learned it), experience (which content or activities will help), and follow-up (what deepens learning after).

quote: I wanted NGSS to work. I believed in it. And then I read the research, and I could not unknow what I had learned.
author: Olivia Mullins
work: NGSS: A Journey From Excitement to Disillusion
expand: Olivia Mullins describes her arc from enthusiastic NGSS adopter to sceptic, driven by her encounter with cognitive science research that undermines the framework's foundational assumptions. The NGSS's guided-inquiry model asks novice students to reason like scientists — to construct explanations from phenomena, engage in argumentation from evidence, and develop models. But as Daniel Willingham's research on the novice-expert distinction demonstrates, novices lack the organised prior knowledge that makes scientific reasoning possible; without it, open-ended inquiry generates confusion rather than learning. Mullins argues the model is particularly damaging for the most vulnerable learners, who have the least background knowledge to draw on and who benefit most from explicit instruction. She concludes that direct instruction combined with structured hands-on activity is more effective and more equitable than the guided-inquiry model NGSS promotes.

quote: The real work happens in how people interpret that plan, how they interact with each other in response to it, and how those interactions shape what actually changes.
author: Mr. Nickhart
work: Planning for Emergence
expand: Nickhart argues that the standard school improvement planning model—define goals, specify actions, set success criteria, monitor progress—rests on a faulty premise: that complex systems like schools respond to inputs predictably enough for linear cause-and-effect planning to work. As an alternative, he introduces 'planning for emergence'—holding desired outcomes in mind while accepting that improvement will unfold through interpretation and interaction rather than predictable sequencing, because outcomes in complex systems are genuinely difficult to predict even when patterns of behavior are not. The key strategic shift is upstream: away from refining action lists and tightening monitoring, and toward the conditions that shape behavior—trusting relationships, individual intellectual capacity, and organizational capacity to learn. Common SIP actions (deliver CPD, implement consistent strategy, monitor through learning walks) are not wrong in themselves, but they reveal a 'technical problem' mindset that misses the deeper cultural conditions that will determine their success. The leader's role in a complex system is to sustain attention on what is emerging, adapt when the system pushes back, and notice leverage opportunities—spending less time perfecting documents and more time strengthening shared understanding and building the quality of thinking across the organization.

quote: Schools are complex, as opposed to complicated. We can argue that schools are complex, adaptive systems which goes hand in hand with rejecting the notion that most of what we do is merely complicated.
author: Mr. Nickhart
work: Predictably Unpredictable
expand: Mr. Nickhart examines the paradox at the heart of school improvement: schools are complex adaptive systems whose outcomes are unpredictable, yet experienced leaders navigate them partly through pattern recognition. He argues that improvement plans are necessarily written as if cause-and-effect are direct, but that this is a presentation convention, not a description of reality. The real problem arises when leaders begin to believe the neat narrative of their own plans. Drawing on systems thinking principles -- cause and effect are not closely related in time and space; the harder you push, the harder the system pushes back; easy fixes tend to loop back in -- he calls for better pattern recognition in school leaders rather than better plan templates. The article closes by framing June's Leadership Handbook post as an attempt to provide that practical pattern-recognition scaffolding.

quote: Headteachers need to be the gatekeeper of collective attention, managing the expectations of each leader about what is required and when.
author: Mr. Nickhart
work: Questions That a Strategic Plan Must Answer
expand: Nickhart argues that most school improvement plans are too comprehensive because they are structured around Ofsted framework compliance rather than a genuine model of school effectiveness, and proposes five questions that any authentic strategic plan must answer. The five questions are: What are our anchoring concepts of school life? How are we communicating these to the community? What is our shared language? How will the team learn and develop? What needs improving versus what needs maintaining? His preferred anchoring concept is the Ambition Institute's model of school leadership as persistent problem-solving (Tom Rees and Jen Barker), which he argues runs coherently from classroom to system leadership. On shared language, Nickhart argues that the precision of language across tens of colleagues determines whether shared understanding or shared confusion governs daily practice—a strategic infrastructure question that most plans neglect. On team development, he argues that professional learning must be explicitly and deliberately designed across a scale of formality rather than vaguely allocated through INSET days. Critically, he argues headteachers must serve as gatekeepers of collective attention: it is not viable for all middle leaders to simultaneously push improvement in their areas, and deciding what can be maintained at current levels is as strategic as deciding where to improve.

quote: Designing the future before understanding the present can lead to misdiagnosing the school's needs and in turn misdirecting attention and resources.
author: Mr. Nickhart
work: The Anatomy of Strategy
expand: Mr. Nickhart provides a worked example of how he structures school improvement strategy, organised around four foundational concepts: strategic intent, a cohesive hierarchy, culture, and continuous checking. He begins from Vivianne Robinson's caution that designing the future before understanding the present leads to misdirected resources. Strategic intent, in his school's case, is embodied in the 'horizon' metaphor -- continuous improvement toward a moving target -- and three 'horizons': a flourishing school, an evolving curriculum, and serving community. The article maps how strategy, systems, culture-setting, and capacity-building each decompose differently at SLT, subject leader, and year-leader levels, and argues that trust is the prior condition without which none of the above functions.

quote: Most school culture systems fail in the same way. They are designed for one human and burden the other.
author: Chris O'Brien
work: Do It for the Kids and the Adults Too
expand: O'Brien argues that most school culture systems fail because they are designed for one human — either the student or the adult — while burdening the other. Systems built exclusively for students produce aspirational expectations that exhaust adults who must improvise enforcement without tools or language. Systems built exclusively for adults produce clear procedures that manage students without forming them into community. The design move that makes culture systems hold is to explicitly name and serve both humans simultaneously, building experiences that reinforce rather than compete with each other. O'Brien walks through three concrete cases: tiered behavior systems, first days of school, and the reflection room — showing in each case what the student experience and the adult experience look like when designed in parallel versus designed in isolation. The test he offers any school leader is simple: for any given culture subsystem, can you answer without hesitation what the student is experiencing and what the adult is experiencing? Where the answers are vague or the experiences compete, the gap has been found and the redesign cycle should begin there.

quote: The whole job of the summer is to make September boring. Predictable. Designed.
author: Chris O'Brien
work: School Improvement 201: What to Actually Do With Your Summer
expand: Chris O'Brien argues that most summer leadership retreats produce aspiration without implementation, resulting in plans that quietly die by October. The article is a practical framework for principals who have already done the strategic work—set OKRs, named the destination—and now need to translate vision into operational reality before the school year begins. O'Brien structures summer work in three layers: first, aligning leadership teams by translating OKRs into specific systems, behaviors, and dated milestones with clear ownership; second, defining school culture with behavioral precision so that teachers can model and practice the exact routines they will run on Day 1; and third, designing the student experience backward from what a student will feel in the first ninety seconds of the school day. Throughout, he insists that leadership development happens through exposure to ambiguous problems and judgment work, not through decks and OKRs—and that the whole job of summer is to make September 'boring, predictable, designed.' The article is targeted at school leaders but contains transferable principles about the gap between strategy and implementation that apply to any organizational leadership context.

quote: The role itself is undesigned. And you cannot out-discipline an undesigned role any more than your teachers can out-effort an undesigned curriculum. Grit is what you spend when the system is missing.
author: Chris O'Brien
work: The Last System Youll Ever Build Is Yourself
expand: Chris O'Brien argues that principals who design excellent systems for every part of their school -- hiring, instruction, student culture, communication -- routinely fail to design the one system that determines whether the others survive: themselves. The hero principal operates on heroism and adrenaline, becoming a single point of failure; the systemic principal designs the role itself. O'Brien introduces a 'single point of failure' diagnostic: what breaks the day I'm out sick? The longer that list, the more the principal has become the system rather than building one. He then prescribes the 'Minimize Audit' -- sorting all your responsibilities into Column 1 (only-me work requiring your actual authority) versus Column 2 (only-me-for-now work that should be systematized). Column 2 is not your job; it is a list of systems you haven't built yet. The article argues that indispensability is fragility, and that the real work of a systemic leader is to empty Column 2 so the only work remaining is the high-leverage work that genuinely requires you.

quote: Gee also recommends you move beyond the binary of stay or go options, and consider a third option, like a different lane. Is there a different job within your same school that might not have the same stressors?
author: Ruth Poulsen
work: Should I Stay or Should I Go
expand: Poulsen offers a three-question framework for burned-out educators wrestling with whether to leave their current role. Drawing on Maslach and Leiter's research identifying six burnout sources (workload, control, reward, community, fairness, values mismatch), she argues that focusing only on overwork misses the actual problem in most cases. The first question -- what exactly is causing the burnout? -- requires specific diagnosis before any action makes sense. The second -- what is within your control? -- separates genuine influenceability from structural constraints. The third -- what is your next right step? -- uses Tracey Gee's insight that action leads to clarity to move educators out of rumination into movement, even if small. Poulsen also surfaces a third option beyond stay or go: a different lane in the same school, or the same role with a different supervisor. Research shows employees who feel administratively supported are 70% less likely to burn out -- environment is often more determinative than individual coping strategies. The essay ends with a call not to stay out of guilt or martyrdom.

quote: In my own burnout experience, I could handle the workload: I couldn't handle the chaos of being repeatedly undermined, and the moral injury of having to act against my values.
author: Regenerative Schools
work: Should I Stay or Should I Go Three Questions to Cut Through the Fog of Burnout
expand: Using Maslach and Leiter's six-factor burnout framework -- workload, control, reward, community, fairness, and values -- the author offers three diagnostic questions for educators experiencing chronic stress: what specifically is causing the burnout, what is within their control, and what is their next right step. The piece argues that most burnout responses focus too narrowly on workload when the actual source may be values misalignment, lack of recognition, community breakdown, or unfairness -- and that misdiagnosing the source leads to ineffective cures. The author shares personal experience of burnout caused not by volume of work but by being repeatedly undermined and required to act against their own values -- naming this as moral injury. Drawing on Tracey Gee's principle that 'action leads to clarity,' the piece advises moving beyond the binary stay-or-go choice to consider a third option: a lateral move to a different role in the same school, or a different school with a more supportive supervisor, noting that employees who feel supported by their administration are 70% less likely to burn out.

quote: Everything I'll show you, an ECT could do tomorrow, with instant impact. You don't have to watch your emotions, and be the adult in the room. You don't have to go out of your way to build great relationships.
author: Dominic Salles
work: Get them to behave with no skills whatsoever Part 3 The Language You Use
expand: This is Part 3 in Salles's practical series on classroom behaviour, providing a toolbox of specific phrases and questions that any teacher can use immediately, regardless of charisma, relationship-building skill, or emotional self-regulation. The article is explicitly not a self-improvement guide — it does not ask teachers to become better people, become trauma-informed, or build deep relationships first — but instead provides a laminated set of tools that produce behavioural improvement from the first day. Salles organises the language into four phases: psychological safety framing (before students start), managing task performance and memory (redirecting during work), eliciting and upgrading thought (after initial responses), and feedback and redirection. The psychological safety language reframes mistakes as the expected and desired state: 'I'm not expecting anyone to get this right first time. That's how we learn' — removing the shame that makes students refuse to engage. The elicitation phase includes high-leverage sequences such as 'Yes, and...' and 'Who can say this even better?' that invite elaboration without threatening the initial respondent. Salles acknowledges the artificiality of some techniques (like tracking the speaker) but explains that what makes students genuinely listen is knowing their opinions matter and that they don't have to agree with the teacher.

quote: The cost of a single Taker on a team — that 30–40% drop in collective performance — dwarfs whatever you gain from hiring a superstar Giver.
author: Dominic Salles
work: How to Create a School of Givers Not Takers
expand: Drawing on Adam Grant's Giver/Taker/Matcher framework, Dominic Salles argues that school culture is shaped more decisively by who is hired than by any programme or initiative. A single Taker — someone who extracts value from colleagues rather than contributing it — can depress team output by 30–40%, a cost that dwarfs even the uplift from a top-performing Giver. Salles warns that the most dangerous hire is not the obvious Taker but the Agreeable Taker: the colleague who appears warm and collegial while quietly hoarding credit and offloading burden. Matchers, who balance giving and taking, tend to mirror the dominant norm — so in a Taker culture they become Takers too, and in a Giver culture they become Givers. The practical implication is that leaders must screen hard for Taker behaviour at the recruitment stage, because cultural remediation after the fact is costly and often fails. Salles closes by noting that the goal is not saintliness but a baseline of generosity that becomes self-sustaining once established.

quote: Effective quality assurance begins with a simple question: what do we need to know in order to improve teaching in this school?
author: Salles, Dominic
work: Quality Assurance that Works
expand: Dominic Salles responds to a Mark Enser post on school quality assurance by proposing a more structured alternative based on his experience leading school improvement. The central argument is that quality assurance too often becomes an exercise in reassurance rather than understanding -- learning walks drift toward surface features, book looks confirm compliance rather than growth, and data stands in for professional judgment -- because it is disconnected from a clear definition of what leaders actually need to know. Salles' preferred alternative is a precise checklist structure: not ambiguous criteria open to subjective interpretation, but specific, universal teaching behaviors that are known to produce learning gains, applied consistently across all subjects. He distinguishes between proxies for teaching and the actual goal (learning), arguing that a small set of well-chosen universal teaching routines -- his school used 'The Essential 8' -- creates conditions for subject leaders, coaches, and teachers to improve in a measurable, cumulative way. The article delivers four concrete checklists covering curriculum quality, lesson visits, book looks, and personal CPD cycles, each grounded in the principle that precision and focus are more powerful than rich but ambiguous frameworks.

quote: Ask your own students, use the scores to celebrate where you are better than national, use the scores to work out how to improve the experience of your students.
author: Dominic Salles
work: Student Voice Is Shocking
expand: A comprehensive national survey of student attitudes reveals significantly low engagement across five critical domains: safety, behavior, academic value, enjoyment, and peer relationships. The data comes from the ImpactEd student voice assessment, a 32-question instrument that measures cognitive engagement (whether students feel safe, perceive academic value, and exercise agency), emotional engagement (enjoyment, worry levels, and sense of belonging), and behavioral engagement (effort and persistence). The findings are sobering: average engagement scores lag well behind what educators would hope. Schools can use this benchmark tool to situate their own student populations, celebrate areas where they exceed national averages, and identify specific improvement targets grounded in evidence. The survey provides a diagnostic window into which dimensions of student experience are strongest and which require intervention.

quote: The verdict on pure, unguided inductive learning was clear: left entirely to their own devices, learners underperformed compared to those receiving direct instruction, with an effect size of d = -0.38.
author: (Santana, Javier)
work: Inductive and Deductive Learning The Power of Guided Discovery
expand: Inductive learning moves from concrete examples to general rules; deductive learning moves from rules to examples. Traditional education is heavily deductive—rules first, examples second—which fails most learners because they are asked to engage with abstract principles before any concrete context has created a need for those principles. A 2011 meta-analysis by Alfieri et al. confirms that pure unguided discovery underperforms direct instruction (effect size d = -0.38), but enhanced inductive learning—with scaffolding, guidance, and explicit conclusions—outperforms it (d = +0.30). The article's central argument is that well-designed inductive learning is not open discovery but carefully curated guided discovery: a show-don't-tell-then-tell structure where selected examples engineer a need to know, and the explicit rule arrives as an answer to a question the learner is already holding. The generation effect amplifies retention—people remember information better when they predict or produce it before receiving the answer, even imperfectly. Practical design formulas (STP, PTP, MTE, CDT) all follow the same underlying logic: create tension or curiosity first, then resolve it with theory. Experts often resist this approach due to the expertise reversal effect, but this resistance reflects their own cognitive efficiency rather than optimal design for beginners. KEY TAKEAWAYS * Inductive learning (concrete to general) outperforms deductive learning (general to concrete) when properly guided—meta-analysis finds effect size d = +0.30 for enhanced inductive vs. traditional instruction. * Pure unguided discovery significantly underperforms (d = -0.38); the power is in guided, scaffolded discovery—good inductive design tells learners exactly where to look. * The need to know is a deliberate engineering goal: start with a carefully chosen example that makes the learner lean in before the rule is revealed. * The generation effect means learners remember information far better when they predict or produce it before receiving the correct version—even wrong guesses deepen retention. * Experts often resist inductive design due to the expertise reversal effect, mistaking their own cognitive efficiency for the optimal learning path for beginners.

quote: With only one student, you would interact with them continually, tuning into how well they're responding to the instruction, noticing successes, uncertainties, errors, gaps in knowledge, subtle issues in their performance.
author: (Sherrington, Tom)
work: The Inherent Challenge of Teaching a Class of Individuals
expand: A thought experiment exposes a structural gap at the heart of classroom teaching: if a teacher had only one student—the least confident in the class—they would interact continuously, track every uncertainty, and never let a learning gap go unaddressed. But when thirty students rejoin the room, none of those individual needs change; what changes is the structural reality of group instruction. It becomes entirely possible for that student to sit through an entire lesson without a single direct engagement from the teacher—and the teacher may not even notice. The student does notice, but because it is their norm, they simply accept it. The article frames this as a brutal reality check: the system's default is to call on volunteers and assume others are following, without any meaningful monitoring of individual progress. Truly inclusive teaching is not the emergent result of caring; it requires deliberate, systematic strategies that structurally involve every learner in every lesson. The key questions every teacher must hold: is there anyone in the room struggling in silence, and how would I even know? KEY TAKEAWAYS * A thought experiment shows that adding 29 students to a one-on-one interaction does not change any individual student's needs—but completely transforms what a teacher can structurally do for them. * It is entirely possible for the least-confident student to experience a full lesson with zero direct engagement from the teacher, while the teacher remains unaware this has happened. * The student accepts this invisibility as normal—their norm—while the teacher's attention flows to volunteers and visible participation. * The system default is not inclusive: it selects from volunteers and assumes others are following, without any teacher knowing how a given individual is actually doing. * Truly inclusive teaching must be deliberately and systematically designed through whole-class techniques; good intentions alone do not produce it.

quote: What matters is not how many minutes are allocated to recess, but how it is designed and plays out in practice.
author: Randy Squier & Mike Petrilli
work: Portrait Is Not a Substitute for Rigor
expand: In this issue of Schooled, Mike Petrilli gives his 'Raise Your Hand' section to Randy Squier, a former New York superintendent, who cautions that 'Portrait of a Graduate' initiatives must complement rigorous academic standards rather than substitute for them. The broader issue also covers state recess mandates (Dale Chu's concern about substituting seat-time inputs for outcome thinking), the AI debate between David Wallace-Wells (skeptical) and Dan Weisberg (optimistic about teacher-support applications), and the civic education landscape. Larry Berger of Amplify explains why the 'science of math' is more complex than the science of reading, requiring both open problem-solving and explicit procedural instruction. Jason Furman and David Laibson defend Harvard's grade inflation cap, acknowledging its imperfections while arguing it solves a collective action problem.

quote: This decision is a confession that the public-facing grading system is, and will remain, theater.
author: (Underwood, Terry)
source: Learning to Read, Reading to Learn (Substack)
work: Too Many A's, Too Few A-'s
expand: Harvard's faculty voted 458-201 to cap A grades at 20% of enrollment per class beginning fall 2027, responding to data showing over half of all undergraduate grades were A's and nearly 85% fell in the A range. The article analyzes this decision through information theory: a letter grade is a maximally lossy compression operator that projects a semester of heterogeneous performance onto a five-point ordinal scale, discarding all qualitative texture by design. When 85% of grades occupy the A range, the symbol has lost its only function—distinguishing position in a distribution—because there is no longer a meaningful not-A to contrast against. The companion measure replacing GPA with average percentile rank for internal honors is, the author argues, an explicit admission that the letter grade was never adequate for comparative ranking work. The cap treats the compressed output as the problem while leaving the upstream cause—changes in instructor judgment over twenty years—completely unaddressed. The author concludes the decision is a public confession that the grading system is, and will remain, theater. KEY TAKEAWAYS * A letter grade is a maximally lossy compression operator—projecting rich performance data onto a five-point ordinal scale, discarding all qualitative information by design. * When 85% of Harvard grades fall in the A range, the letter A has lost its only function: ordinal distinction through contrast with a meaningful not-A. * The companion measure switching internal honors to percentile rank is an implicit admission that the letter grade was always inadequate for comparative ranking. * The cap treats the compressed output signal as the problem while leaving the upstream cause—changes in instructor judgment over decades—completely unaddressed. * The decision amounts to a public confession that the external-facing grading system is, and will remain, theater while a real ranking system operates underneath.

quote: Seventeen states have banned three-cueing instruction. Three-cueing is still being used in seventeen states. The ban reached the statehouse. It has not yet reached Room 214.
author: Karen Vaites
work: The New Fordham Report Is Exhibit 729 for Policy Is Not Progress
expand: Karen Vaites examines the Thomas B. Fordham Institute's latest Science of Reading progress report and finds a familiar pattern: measurable gains in teacher knowledge have not translated into measurable gains in classroom practice. Teachers trained in UFLI-style structured literacy now know more about phonemic awareness and decoding — but Benchmark Assessment System remains on state-approved curriculum lists despite being classified as 'unscientific' by independent reviewers, and three-cueing persists in classrooms across 17 states that have technically banned it. Vaites argues the report is Exhibit 729 in the ongoing case that policy and progress are not the same thing. Passing a law changes the legal landscape; it does not change what a teacher does at 9:15 on a Tuesday. The article calls for a shift from policy advocacy to implementation science.

quote: To be able to make an inference, for example, you need a certain threshold of relevant background knowledge. Lots of kids lack enough background knowledge to make sense of the passages on reading tests.
author: Natalie Wexler
work: Is Testing the Next Frontier in Ed Reform Louisiana Experiment
expand: Wexler examines Louisiana's Innovative Assessment (IA), an experiment grounding reading tests in taught content rather than generic skills. Though discontinued in 2024 after only two full years, the IA showed promise: students felt less anxious, teachers shifted from isolated skills to whole-text meaning-making, and gaps between disadvantaged and affluent students narrowed. However, changing state tests requires coordination across bureaucratic silos—curriculum officials, test designers, legislatures—and the political will rarely materializes. The article argues that what gets tested gets taught, making content-aligned assessment crucial for knowledge-building reform.

quote: Expeditionary education works because coherence is built into the structure, not added on.
author: Patrick Whalen
work: Expeditionary Education
expand: Expeditionary education—learning through extended investigations and field-based experiences—offers a coherent alternative to traditional instruction. Rather than fragmented lessons, students engage in sustained projects that require applying knowledge from multiple domains to real investigations. The power of this approach lies not in the novelty of outdoor education but in the structural coherence: students learn mathematics because calculations are required for their expedition; they develop writing because documentation is necessary; they engage in science because investigation demands it. This intrinsic motivation combined with forced integration of knowledge creates learning conditions superior to both traditional classroom instruction and disconnected enrichment activities. However, expeditionary education requires different school organization and teacher preparation than conventional schooling.

quote: The pleasure of effort comes from seeing progress toward something that matters.
author: Patrick Whalen
work: The Pleasure of Effort
expand: Effort itself can be rewarding, but schools have systematically removed conditions that make effort enjoyable and replaced them with meaningless difficulty. Students work hardest when their effort produces visible progress toward goals they care about—solving problems, building understanding, creating something valuable. When schools extract effort from purpose—asking students to work hard on meaningless tasks—they disconnect effort from its psychological reward. The result is students who avoid effort and schools that interpret this as laziness rather than rational response to poor incentive structures. Reconnecting effort to meaningful outcome requires redesigning what students work on, not simply demanding more motivation.

quote: In other words, they not only explain the problem or opportunity set but also ask for the team’s help in challenging their working assumptions.
author: Admired Leadership Field Notes
expand: The feedback is one of the most common pieces of 360-review language a fast-moving leader receives, and the authors make the useful move of splitting it in two. There are actually two distinct problems hiding inside "you don't bring people along." The first is communicating the rationale behind the leader's thinking and task requests. The second is gaining subscription to the resulting plan, usually through inclusion in the work of shaping it. The piece focuses on the first because subscription has been covered in earlier Field Notes, and because the communication failure is the one that most often catches high-capacity leaders by surprise. The diagnostic is sharp. Facile leaders who, like sharks, are in constant motion tend to run several steps ahead of the team before anyone else has seen the problem. That speed is a genuine strength until the leader fails to stop and say what they are seeing, what strategy they are forming, and what reasoning underlies the requests being handed down. Team members asked to execute on conclusions they do not understand either execute without rigor or intentionally drag their feet because the absence of explanation reads as disrespect. Over time, uncertainty becomes disengagement and rigor drops out of the work. The corrective is two-part and deceptively simple. Tell the team what you are seeing, what you are thinking, and why, before any plan takes shape. Then invite them to probe the evidence and the reasoning rather than debate the conclusions. The move is not to surrender the decision, it is to open the working assumptions to challenge from the people who will have to execute against them. Good leaders bring people along by making their reasoning audible and inspectable, which is the work that closes the gap between a fast leader and a team that can actually keep pace.

quote: Without explanatory depth, school improvement plans are just words on a page; random rabbits pulled out of a hat.
author: Matthew Evans
work: The Hollow Plan
expand: Matthew Evans argues that most school improvement plans are 'hollow' because they identify what leaders want to change without explaining why things are as they are or how the proposed change will actually make things better. The Ofsted self-evaluation process has been corrupted by deficit frameworks that essentially write improvement plans automatically -- priorities being whatever criteria are not yet ticked off on the inspection checklist. Evans identifies two critical types of explanation that hollow plans lack: a causal theory (why things are as they are) and a theory of change (how proposed actions will actually improve outcomes). Without these, improvement priorities are conjuring tricks. The article uses a punctuality problem as a worked example to show how weak causal theories lead to misguided interventions, and argues that brave headteachers must resist the temptation to let Ofsted frameworks do their thinking for them.

quote: I'd argue that action planning is the artefact of the school improvement layer and that systems and processes are far more important artefacts at the habit layer.
author: Nick Hart
work: Not Everyone Needs an Action Plan
expand: Nick Hart extends his three-layer school improvement model -- base layer (horizon habits), middle layer (improvement plan), top layer (strategic direction) -- to argue that action planning is the artefact of the middle layer only, and that leaders working at the base layer should be building systems and timelines rather than writing plans. At the base layer, leaders are reinforcing established ways of working, addressing within-school variance, and prioritising coherence over consistency -- ensuring that shared principles are applied thoughtfully across different contexts rather than demanding identical practice. Hart argues that most teacher learning is informal, happening through day-to-day collaboration rather than formal courses, so the artefact that matters most at the base layer is a timeline that helps leaders organise and prioritise collaborative activity. He illustrates this with a worked example for a Head of Year, contrasting two approaches to planning meetings: one that delegates planning upstream and then asks 'any questions?' and one that structures the meeting to build collective knowledge through guided discussion of specific decisions. The article is addressed to school leaders who default to action-planning for every problem regardless of which layer of improvement work is actually required, arguing that the act of writing an action plan can itself become a substitute for the more important work of building the habits and collaborative structures that improvement actually requires.

quote: Every move away from students is treated as advancement. Every move closer to students is treated as regression.
author: Jolein
work: Burn the Ladder
expand: Jolein, a district-level education leader, argues that the conventional career ladder in education -- from teacher to coach to assistant principal to principal to district leader -- is a dangerous mental model that equates upward movement with advancement and proximity to students with regression. The piece opens with the author's own admission that after years away from school buildings, they had lost sharpness -- and instead of hiding from it, they increased their reps (covering schools, being present in buildings). The core concept is 'muscular humility' (Daniel Coyle): a leadership posture that finds simple ways to serve the group and signals 'we are all in this together,' insisting 'I am not above practicing.' The ladder makes leaders brittle: when identity is tied only to one role or salary band, any disruption becomes existential, producing leaders who protect their image rather than tell the truth. The essay calls for leaders to build identities that work across roles -- to find worth in the work itself rather than in the title.

quote: You cannot build leadership structures on top of a profession that has been systematically taught to doubt its own judgment.
author: Jo Lein
work: You Cannot Build Leadership on Top of Professional Doubt
expand: Jo Lein argues that educational leadership development programs often fail because they are built on a profession that has been systematically trained to doubt its own judgment. Coaching and instructional support -- delivered through over-prescription, over-observation without affirmation, 'gotcha' questioning, and data-as-weapon -- inadvertently erode teacher agency rather than build it. Lein identifies six subtle coaching behaviors that create professional doubt and describes the precise moment most leaders miss: the window when improvement happens but goes unacknowledged. The antidote is not structural but relational: leaders must actively rebuild professional judgment by asking genuine questions, noticing growth, and helping teachers see data with curiosity rather than dread. Leadership and professional judgment must be built simultaneously -- you cannot have one without the other.

quote: Schools are complex adaptive systems; networks of people, relationships and beliefs that interact in ways that cannot be fully predicted or controlled.
author: Mr. Nickhart
work: A SIP Isnt Enough
expand: Mr. Nickhart argues that a School Improvement Plan (SIP) is not the starting point for school improvement planning -- it is the output of a prior, more fundamental question: what is our theory of change? Before deciding what goes on a plan, leaders need views on what kind of thing a school is, how they can reliably know reality, how improvement happens, and how to choose what to focus on. Drawing on complexity science, Nickhart argues that schools are complex adaptive systems where outcomes emerge unpredictably from conditions rather than being produced directly by actions -- meaning school improvement is a 'wicked problem' with no linear cause-and-effect. A plan without a theory of change is a collection of data fields with no coherent logic connecting them.

quote: There is a good chance that, when we look at things going on at department or classroom level and are not happy with what we find, we are seeing the symptoms of a problem further upstream, rather than the cause of the problem.
author: Mr. Nickhart
work: Purpose
expand: Mr. Nickhart applies the Desmond Tutu upstream/downstream metaphor to school leadership, arguing that when leaders spend disproportionate time quality-assuring classroom outputs (pulling people out of the river), it is because the upstream problem -- the absence of clear, shared purpose -- has not been addressed. Without purpose, leaders can only manage the downstream because there is nothing else to anchor attention. Purpose is not a mission statement but a set of interlocking elements: aims and values, strategic priorities, and the specific curriculum and pedagogical decisions that flow from them. When purpose is well-defined, communicated, and understood, colleagues know what to prioritize in the limited time they have to influence what children learn -- and quality assurance becomes an upstream investment rather than downstream firefighting.

quote: This is what a broken Communication System looks like. Not because nobody communicated. Because the principal was the communication system.
author: Chris O'Brien
work: Communication Is Everything. Everything Is Communication.
expand: Chris O'Brien (adapted from 'The Systemic Principal') opens with a Thursday parent conference scenario -- five teachers leave early or stay late based on different hallway answers, a family arrives for a missed appointment -- to show what a broken communication system looks like. The problem is not that nobody communicated; it is a design problem. When a school has no designed Communication System, the principal becomes one by default, creating unequal information environments where those with proximity to the principal know what is happening and everyone else fills the gap with rumor, assumption, and fear. The solution is a four-subsystem Communication System: (1) Weekly Rhythm -- Wednesday leadership team meeting where decisions are made and action steps are owned, plus Friday staff email where the week becomes legible to everyone at the same time; (2) Communication Cascade -- information reaches leaders before staff and staff before families, so leaders can be stewards of the message; (3) Principal's Voice -- communication only the principal can make (vision, hard truths, change announcements), protected by restraint; (4) Feedback Architecture -- designed upward channels including the 24/48 rule (if still bothering you 24 hours later, address it directly within 48, then let it go). Together, the four subsystems produce a school where the principal stops being the communication system and becomes the leader of one. Key Points * When a school has no designed Communication System, the principal becomes one by default -- creating an information environment where proximity to the principal determines what staff know, and the gap fills with rumor and fear. * The Weekly Rhythm (Wednesday leadership meeting + Friday staff email) ensures the hallway question never has to happen: the answer was already in the email, the decision was already in the meeting. * The Communication Cascade -- leaders briefed before staff, staff before families -- protects leaders' ability to steward the message rather than receive it alongside their teams. * The Principal's Voice is what only the principal can say: vision, hard truths, change announced in language that holds. It loses weight when deployed too often; restraint is the design choice that protects it. * Feedback Architecture designs upward channels rather than leaving them informal -- the 24/48 rule: if still bothering you 24 hours later, address directly with that person within 48 hours; after that, let it go.

quote: We do not learn from experience. We learn from reflecting on experience.
author: Regenerative Schools
work: The Most Important End-of-Year Task
expand: Using sandhill cranes' migratory cycles as a metaphor, Regenerative Schools argues that June reflection is the most important end-of-year task an educator or school leader can perform -- and the one they should never cut even when everything else is being pruned. The article provides a structured reflection protocol -- Toss, Tweak, Treasure -- from the Institute for Social and Emotional Learning, along with specific prompt categories for school leaders: pruning the task list, building buffers for thinking, culture of well-being, trust-building, collaboration, and culture of agency. It then argues for vulnerable transparency: sharing some of what you discovered in reflection with your team. Leaders who model hard work and rest, reflection and transparency, build regenerative schools that can sustain improvement across cycles.

quote: We may be born with the ingredients of systems thinking. But we are not born with the discipline of systems thinking.
author: Nicole (The Critical Path / PM Researcher)
work: The Reader Comment That Sent Me Down a Systems Thinking Rabbit Hole
expand: A reader's question -- whether some people naturally think in systems -- sent Nicole of The Critical Path into a research deep-dive on the cognitive origins of systems thinking. She finds that humans are born with causal curiosity and pattern-recognition instincts (the raw ingredients of systems thinking) but argues these are not the same as systems thinking itself. The key distinction is between holistic thinking (a natural tendency to sense relationships) and systems thinking (a disciplined practice that can map, test, and act on those relationships). Research on children's causal reasoning and relational reasoning suggests that the ability to transfer patterns across situations develops gradually with age, hinting that systems thinking can be built. The article concludes that treating systems thinking as a personality trait wastes its potential, while treating it as a teachable capability transforms how teams diagnose recurring project problems.

quote: If you force a student to draw a specific bar model, a rectangle or a fraction wall every single time they solve a problem, you have not taught the concept. You have simply taught a drawing algorithm.
author: (Brendan)
work: Why the Conceptual Understanding Debate Is Arguing Over the Wrong Premise
expand: The debate between procedural fluency and conceptual understanding has stalled because neither side has a precise vocabulary for what a concept actually is. Drawing on Tiemann and Markle's taxonomy, the article defines a concept as a mental category with Critical Attributes (must-haves) and Variable Attributes (can-haves): concept learning has occurred only when a student can correctly classify a completely novel, unseen instance. This framework clarifies the most common instructional error—the proceduralization of concepts—which occurs when a concept-teaching tool like a bar model is turned into a mandatory drawing algorithm, teaching a new procedure rather than the underlying category. It also clarifies valid concept assessment: tests that ask students to respond to new instances, not reproduce familiar diagrams. The Instructional Hierarchy of Haring and Eaton provides the developmental sequence: concept acquisition must precede fluency, which precedes generalization; collapsing these stages produces the problems both sides of the debate diagnose. Procedural and conceptual understanding are genuinely different cognitive architectures requiring different instructional designs, and the Tiemann-Markle framework makes both teachable and assessable with precision. KEY TAKEAWAYS * A concept is a mental category defined by Critical Attributes (must-haves) and Variable Attributes (can-haves); concept learning occurs only when a student classifies novel, unseen instances correctly. * Proceduralizing concepts—turning a concept-teaching tool into a mandatory step-by-step routine—teaches a new procedure, not the underlying category. * Valid concept assessment asks students to respond to new instances, not define or reproduce familiar diagrams; the latter tests only memorized verbal algorithms. * Procedural fluency and conceptual understanding are different cognitive architectures requiring different instructional designs—conflating them is the source of most classroom confusion in this debate. * The Instructional Hierarchy requires acquisition to precede fluency, which precedes generalization—collapsing these stages produces exactly the problems both sides of the debate are trying to solve.

quote: What we fail to realize is that we just aren't good at it yet, and no one likes doing something they aren't good at unless they can see a light at the end of the tunnel.
author: (Cauthen, Colton)
work: They Didn't Teach You How To Read
expand: Literacy and reading are not the same thing. Most people are taught to decode symbols into meaning but not to read in the fuller sense, which involves progressive mastery across Mortimer Adler's four levels: Elementary, Inspectional, Analytical, and Syntopical. The article argues that treating reading as a binary skill—you either can or cannot—rather than a developable complex skill causes three predictable harms: people conclude they dislike reading when they are simply not yet skilled enough; they plateau because they believe there is nothing more to develop; and they mistake volume for depth, exemplified by listening to hundreds of books at 2.5x speed and calling it reading. Like chess or surgery, the skill of reading admits degrees of mastery that most people never approach—and most do not realize this. The good news is that recognizing reading as a cultivatable skill reverses all three harms, opening a path toward a richer reading life built through discipline, curiosity, and good practice. KEY TAKEAWAYS * Literacy (decoding symbols) and reading in the full analytical sense are different skills; most people are taught the former and never develop the latter. * Adler's four-level hierarchy—Elementary, Inspectional, Analytical, Syntopical—frames reading as a progressively complex skill at which most people plateau at level one without realizing it. * Treating reading as binary rather than developmental causes people to conclude they dislike it when they are simply not yet skilled enough to experience its rewards. * The volume-hype reading scene—hundreds of books per year at 2.5x speed—mistakes breadth for depth and passive consumption for active, analytical engagement. * Recognizing reading as a cultivatable skill reverses all three harms: reframes frustration as a growth stage, reveals the plateau, and redirects attention from volume to quality.

quote: Schools that depend on brilliance or burnout eventually run out of both.
author: Findlater, Sarah
work: A More Sustainable Way Forward What Teaching Could Feel Like Next
expand: Final post in the 16-part teaching wellbeing series. Teacher burnout is not about lacking resilience but about structurally unsustainable work. Sustainability means designing work humans can do well over time: bounded expectations, systems that reduce friction, protected recovery time. AI can help if used to reduce repetition and offload low-value tasks, not to accelerate pace. Real change requires system design and leadership modeling, not individual mindset shifts.

quote: For people in the trenches, I'd encourage them to remember that student learning depends on student effort and teacher support.
author: Peter Greene
work: Reformster Nostalgia and New Old Mistakes
expand: Education reformers are experiencing nostalgia for Bush-Obama era bipartisan consensus on testing, choice, and accountability. Mike Petrilli notes that reform fatigue resulted from two decades of push-back and public exhaustion with high-stakes testing. However, Greene argues reform failed not from fatigue but because it didn't work. High-stakes testing narrowed curriculum, damaged schools, and produced no academic gains while serving data-tech overlords and privatization advocates. True reform would ask how to make schools better, not how to inject market mechanisms. Including actual teachers in reform conversations would ground proposals in classroom reality.

quote: Common Core treated reading as a skill rather than a practice: a transferable ability that could be exercised on any passage. If that’s true, any passage will do, and short passages are cheaper to write, align, and test.
author: Peter Greene
work: Whole Books and Slow School
expand: Greene traces the decline of whole-book reading in American schools to structural forces: Common Core's skill-heavy frame and testing regimes that reward speed over depth. When reading is treated as a transferable skill operable on any text, short passages become cheaper and easier to align; the result is steady substitution of excerpts for books. Whole books uniquely allow characters, ideas, and language to develop, train sustained attention, and invite reader-response conversations that require shared imaginative worlds. Greene extends the case to a broader principle of slow school: mathematics benefits from time with hard problems, history from long primary sources, writing from drafts that mature across weeks. The call is to resist speed-maximising assumptions and protect the experiences that make educated people.

quote: Others are integrators, weaving understanding across connected contexts
author: Nick Hart
work: Architects and Integrators
expand: Teachers fall into two pedagogical orientations: architects who design comprehensive knowledge structures and integrators who weave knowledge across contexts. Both have pedagogical merit. Architects build systematic, sequenced understanding. Integrators connect learning to real-world contexts. Excellence requires recognizing both approaches and understanding when each serves students best.

quote: Any meeting of time-poor teachers needs to be purposeful. The best use of time is to focus on the substance of subject content, pedagogy, misconceptions, task design and adaptation.
author: Hart, Nick
work: Collaborative Planning Meetings
expand: Hart outlines how collaborative planning meetings can improve teaching quality when focused on substance: subject content sequencing, pedagogy, misconceptions, task design, and adaptation. He warns against tokenistic sharing and emphasizes planning at least two weeks ahead to allow time for team feedback. Leadership teams must strategically choose which subjects to examine in depth based on data and teacher needs.
